GS-AIPS-ACCEPTAB-01.title: GovSecure — Enterprise AI Acceptable Use Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Sets the rules for day-to-day employee use of AI tools and AI-generated outputs, including approved use, prohibited use, data-entry restrictions, output review expectations, and accountability.
Applies to all personnel using enterprise-approved AI tools or AI-assisted outputs for company business, including pilots and embedded SaaS AI capabilities.
All employees, contractors, temporary workers, and approved third parties using enterprise AI tools

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: Only approved AI tools and approved use cases may be used for business purposes. Employees shall not use consumer or unapproved AI tools for company work unless an exception is formally approved.
Users shall not input restricted or prohibited data into AI tools unless the specific tool, use case, and handling method have been reviewed and approved for that data category.

AI-generated outputs must be reviewed by a human before being relied upon for business decisions, customer communications, code deployment, contractual language, financial analysis, or regulated activities.
AI may be used to assist work, but accountability for final content, actions, and outcomes remains with the employee and the business owner.
AI shall not be used to create deceptive, discriminatory, harassing, unlawful, or manipulative content, or to impersonate individuals without authorization.
Users must follow enterprise copyright, privacy, records retention, security, and communications requirements when using AI-generated content.
AI outputs that materially affect customers, employees, applicants, or business commitments must include human verification and, where appropriate, documented approval.
Prompt histories, uploads, generated files, and AI-assisted work products shall be handled as business records when they support decisions, approvals, or regulated activities.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: AI Governance Lead maintains the approved AI tools list, usage rules, and exception process.
Managers ensure personnel understand approved use boundaries and complete required training.
Users remain accountable for the accuracy, legality, and appropriateness of AI-assisted work products.
Security, Privacy, and Legal provide review support for restricted data, sensitive outputs, and exceptions.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: Approved AI tools register
Employee acknowledgement record
Training completion evidence
Exception request log
Output review evidence for high-impact use cases

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: Approved AI Tools Register
Employee AI Usage Acknowledgment Form
Prohibited Use Exception Request Form
AI Output Human Review Checklist
AI Training Attendance Log
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

sections.heading: Implementation Note
paragraphs: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-DATAPRIV-02.title: GovSecure — AI Data Handling and Privacy Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Defines what data may be used in AI systems, what data is restricted or prohibited, and how AI-related data must be classified, retained, deleted, transferred, and protected.
Applies to all AI systems that process company, customer, employee, or third-party data, including prompts, uploads, outputs, logs, and retrieval content.
Employees using AI, data owners, system owners, Privacy, Security, Legal, and procurement teams

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: Data entered into AI systems shall be classified and handled in accordance with enterprise data classification and privacy requirements.
Restricted, sensitive, personal, regulated, or customer-confidential data may only be processed in approved AI systems that are explicitly authorized for those data categories.
AI vendors and internal AI solutions shall disclose data retention, deletion, subprocessors, regional processing, human review access, and training use of submitted data.
The enterprise shall prohibit use of AI systems that reuse customer or employee data for model training without explicit authorization and documented approval.
Cross-border transfers, regional processing, and remote support access involving AI systems must follow enterprise privacy, contractual, and transfer requirements.
Prompts, uploads, outputs, and AI logs that contain personal or sensitive information shall be retained only as long as necessary for the approved purpose and then securely deleted or archived.
The enterprise shall support privacy impact review for AI use cases involving personal data, profiling, monitoring, or decisions affecting individuals.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Privacy Lead defines privacy requirements, review triggers, and data protection expectations for AI use.
Data owners classify information and approve use of data categories in AI systems.
System owners ensure approved retention, deletion, transfer, and regional controls are implemented.
Security and Procurement validate vendor commitments for data handling, subprocessors, and access.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: Privacy impact assessments
Data retention schedule
Cross-border transfer review records
Vendor privacy commitments
Sensitive data exception approvals

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: AI Data Classification Guide
Sensitive Data Submission Restriction Notice
AI Privacy Impact Assessment Form
AI Data Retention and Deletion Schedule
Cross-Border AI Data Transfer Review Form
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-GOVERNAN-03.title: GovSecure — AI Governance Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Defines the governance structure for AI across the enterprise, including ownership, oversight, approval authority, escalation, risk accountability, and review responsibilities.
Applies to all enterprise AI systems, AI use cases, embedded AI capabilities, significant model/provider changes, and related governance forums and records.
Executive leadership, business owners, Security, Privacy, Legal, IT, Procurement, Risk, and Compliance teams

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: The enterprise shall maintain a formal AI governance structure with defined ownership, decision rights, escalation paths, and oversight responsibilities.
Every AI system and AI use case shall have an accountable business owner and an identified technical or platform owner.
New AI use cases, major changes, and exceptions shall be reviewed through the enterprise AI intake and risk assessment process before approval.
The AI Governance Lead shall maintain the AI system inventory, governance standards, review cadence, and reporting to leadership.
High-risk or sensitive AI use cases shall receive enhanced review involving Security, Privacy, Legal, and relevant business stakeholders before deployment.
The enterprise shall review AI risks, control gaps, incidents, and exceptions on a periodic basis and track remediation to closure.
No AI initiative may bypass required governance merely because the capability is bundled into an existing SaaS product or business workflow.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Executive Sponsor provides direction, resolves escalations, and approves material risk decisions.
AI Governance Lead runs the intake process, inventory, review cadence, and governance reporting.
Business owners are accountable for approved use cases, outcomes, and operational controls.
Security, Privacy, Legal, IT, and Procurement support reviews based on risk tier and change impact.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: AI governance charter
RACI matrix
AI intake and approval records
Quarterly governance review deck
Open risk and exception tracker

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: AI Governance Charter
AI Governance RACI Matrix
AI Steering Committee Terms of Reference
AI Escalation Path Matrix
Quarterly AI Governance Review Deck
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-HUMANOVE-04.title: GovSecure — Human Oversight and Decision-Making Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Defines when human review is required before AI outputs are acted upon, especially for legal, financial, employment, safety, health, and customer-impacting decisions.
Applies to AI-supported activities where outputs may influence legal, financial, employment, safety, customer, regulatory, or other high-impact decisions.
Business decision-makers, managers, operations teams, HR, Legal, compliance reviewers, and customer-facing teams

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: AI may support human decision-making but shall not replace required human judgment for high-impact, regulated, or rights-affecting decisions unless explicitly approved by policy and law.
High-impact outputs must be reviewed by a qualified human who has authority to approve, reject, escalate, or override the AI-assisted recommendation.
Business owners shall define what qualifies as a high-impact use case, what review steps are mandatory, and what evidence must be retained.
Where AI outputs are uncertain, incomplete, or ambiguous, the workflow shall route to human escalation rather than proceed silently.
Teams shall not present AI-generated conclusions as authoritative without appropriate review, context, and, where necessary, transparency to affected parties.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Business owners define which workflows are high-impact and what human review steps are mandatory.
Qualified reviewers validate AI outputs before decisions, communications, or actions proceed.
Teams document overrides, escalations, and validation evidence for high-impact use cases.
AI Governance, Legal, and Compliance provide guidance where regulated, sensitive, or ambiguous outcomes exist.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: Human review checklist
Decision approval record
Override log
Escalation records
Output validation samples

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: Human-in-the-Loop Review Checklist
High-Impact Decision Approval Form
AI Output Validation Record
Escalation and Override Log
AI-Assisted Decision Review Guide
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-INCIDENT-05.title: GovSecure — AI Incident Response and Monitoring Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Defines what qualifies as an AI incident and how the organization detects, escalates, investigates, contains, remediates, and learns from AI-related failures or harms.
Applies to monitoring, triage, escalation, containment, investigation, remediation, and post-incident review for AI-related events and control failures.
Security operations, Privacy, Legal, IT, risk teams, executives, incident responders, and system owners

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: The enterprise shall monitor approved AI systems for misuse, access anomalies, material failures, drift, harmful outputs, data leakage, and other indicators of elevated risk as feasible for the system type.

AI incidents shall be triaged using the enterprise incident management process with AI-specific classifications for privacy harm, security exposure, model failure, unsafe automation, vendor failure, and harmful content.
Material AI incidents shall be escalated promptly to Security, Privacy, Legal, business owners, and leadership based on severity and impact.
The enterprise shall document containment, remediation, root cause, customer or stakeholder impact, and corrective actions for material AI incidents.
Monitoring, incident trends, unresolved issues, and required improvements shall be reviewed periodically as part of governance reporting.
Vendor-caused incidents and external service changes that materially affect risk posture shall trigger review and, where necessary, suspension of the affected use case or provider.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Information Security leads AI incident triage, containment, investigation, and corrective action tracking.
Privacy and Legal support incidents involving personal data, regulatory exposure, or stakeholder harm.
Business and platform owners support technical analysis, remediation, and communications planning.
Leadership reviews material incidents, open issues, and recurring trends through the governance process.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: Monitoring standard
Incident response playbook
Incident intake forms
Root cause analysis
Corrective action tracker

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: AI Logging and Monitoring Standard
AI Incident Response Playbook
AI Incident Intake Form
AI Harm and Impact Assessment Sheet
Post-Incident Corrective Action Tracker
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-RISKAPPR-06.title: GovSecure — AI Risk Assessment and Use-Case Approval Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Requires AI use cases to be reviewed before adoption, procurement, deployment, or material change, and establishes risk tiers, approval triggers, prohibited uses, and enhanced review criteria.
Applies to all new AI use cases, major changes to approved AI solutions, and any exceptions, including internally built and third-party AI capabilities.
Business owners, project sponsors, AI requestors, procurement, IT, Security, Privacy, and Legal

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: All new AI use cases shall be submitted through the enterprise AI intake process before deployment, procurement, or broad internal release.
Each use case shall be assigned a risk tier based on data sensitivity, decision impact, user population, autonomy, transparency needs, and vendor or technical dependencies.
High-risk, sensitive, or regulated use cases shall require enhanced review and documented approvals from relevant control functions before go-live.
Use cases involving employment, finance, legal, safety, health, minors, profiling, or high-impact customer decisions shall be presumed elevated risk unless documented otherwise.
Material changes to approved AI systems, including new data sources, new actions, new integrations, or model/provider changes, shall trigger reassessment.
Prohibited use cases shall not proceed without explicit executive approval and formal exception documentation.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Requestors submit new AI use cases and material changes before implementation or expansion.
AI Governance Lead manages triage, risk tiering, approval routing, and exception handling.
Reviewers from Security, Privacy, Legal, and the business participate based on risk level and use case type.
Business owners may not deploy high-risk use cases until required approvals and safeguards are documented.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: AI use case intake form
Risk tiering worksheet
Approval decision record
Enhanced review notes
Exception register

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: AI Use Case Intake Form
AI Risk Tiering Worksheet
AI Approval Decision Record
High-Risk AI Review Checklist
AI Exception Request Form
Implementation note: This policy should be approved by the client’s designated owner, linked to operating procedures, and reviewed at least annually or after a material AI-related change.

GS-AIPS-SECURITY-07.title: GovSecure — AI Security Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Defines security requirements for enterprise AI tools, models, APIs, agents, integrations, credentials, logging, and access, including controls for misuse, prompt injection, and data leakage.
Applies to enterprise AI systems, connectors, APIs, agentic features, retrieval layers, administrative access, and all supporting technical integrations.
Security teams, IT administrators, developers, engineers, platform owners, and privileged AI capability users

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: AI systems shall implement least-privilege access, appropriate authentication, approved identity integrations, and access review practices commensurate with risk.
Secrets, API keys, service accounts, connectors, and retrieval stores used by AI systems shall be protected, monitored, and managed in accordance with enterprise security standards.
Logging shall be enabled for administrative access, privileged actions, connector use, material configuration changes, and AI incidents or suspected misuse where technically feasible.
AI systems that can take actions, access enterprise repositories, or influence workflows shall implement scoped permissions, testing, and rollback or disablement capabilities.
Prompt injection, data leakage, model abuse, cross-tenant exposure, and unsafe retrieval risks shall be addressed through layered security controls and periodic validation.
No AI integration may bypass approved security architecture, network, endpoint, API, or secrets-management controls.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Information Security defines baseline safeguards for access, logging, integration, secrets, and testing.
IT and platform owners implement approved configurations and maintain secure operational controls.
Developers and integrators follow secure design requirements for prompts, APIs, connectors, and agents.
Business owners may not enable high-risk AI capabilities without required security review and sign-off.

sections.heading: Required Records and Evidence
bullets: Security architecture review
Access review evidence
Logging standard
Penetration or abuse testing results
Incident intake records

sections.heading: Required Companion Templates
bullets: AI Security Control Baseline
AI Access Control Review Form
AI API and Integration Security Checklist
Prompt Injection and Abuse Testing Checklist
AI Security Incident Intake Form

sections.heading: GovSecure AI Policy Suite Map
paragraphs: 15 Core Enterprise AI Policies with Purpose, Ownership, Audience, and Companion Templates
Version 1.0
tables: Designed to help organizations structure enterprise AI governance, privacy, security, risk review, and operational accountability.
Recommended use: policy architecture, client delivery, governance planning, product packaging, and implementation workshops.

sections.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Purpose. This document defines the primary enterprise AI policies recommended for a formal AI governance program and maps each policy to its purpose, primary owner, intended audience, and companion templates required for operational use.

How to use this document. Use this suite map as a policy architecture guide, a client-facing planning document, or the backbone for a GovSecure policy product. It is designed to move organizations from loose AI guidance to a structured, accountable, and implementation-ready policy set.

Recommended packaging tiers. Tier 1 covers the starter set for organizations beginning formal AI governance. Tier 2 adds operational control depth. Tier 3 adds maturity, assurance, and audit readiness.

Sets requirements for evaluating AI vendors and service providers.

sections.heading: GovSecure Productization Recommendation
paragraphs: Recommended product name. GovSecure AI Policy Suite Map - 15 Core Enterprise AI Policies with Operational Templates

Commercial packaging approach. Offer the suite in layers: Lite (policy map + core templates), Standard (8 drafted policies + template pack), and Pro (full 15-policy suite + questionnaires + registry + evidence pack).
Positioning note. The value is not only in the policy documents themselves but in the operational templates that make the policies usable, reviewable, and auditable.

sections.heading: Closing Note
paragraphs: This suite map is designed to help organizations stand up an AI governance program that is practical, role-aware, and scalable. It can serve as a planning document, a client-ready advisory deliverable, or the backbone of a GovSecure product offering.

paragraphs: Single-offering package of core policies, supporting standards, forms, questionnaire, registry, and charter

sections.heading: What is included
paragraphs: This package combines the core policy layer already completed with the operational artifacts needed to turn policy into day-to-day governance. It is designed as a paid SMB-friendly offering that can be adopted internally or delivered to clients as a practical AI governance starter set.

sections.heading: Package structure
paragraphs: • 00_Overview — offering guide and usage notes

sections.heading: Recommended deployment sequence

• Layer in the supporting standards and procedures for monitoring, changes, disclosure, and evidence retention once the initial intake and inventory process is running.

GS-AIPS-THIRDPAR-11.title: GovSecure — Third-Party / Vendor AI Due Diligence Policy — Standalone Branded Policy Document

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: Practical governance beats vague good intentions.
Use this document as a client-ready policy baseline. Customize named roles, escalation paths, and references to internal standards, classifications, and regulatory obligations before approval.

sections.heading: Policy Overview
tables: Establishes the requirements for assessing third-party AI vendors, platforms, models, and service providers before onboarding, renewal, or major scope change.
Applies to third-party AI vendors, model providers, copilots, SaaS features with embedded AI, implementation partners, and material vendor changes.
Procurement, Vendor Management, Security, Privacy, Legal, business sponsors, and AI governance reviewers

sections.heading: Policy Statements
bullets: Third-party AI vendors shall be assessed before onboarding, renewal, or material change using privacy, security, legal, operational, and governance criteria appropriate to the proposed use case.
No vendor may be approved without clarity on training use of customer data, retention, deletion, subprocessors, regional processing, incident obligations, and human review access where applicable.
Vendors supporting sensitive, regulated, or high-impact use cases shall provide enhanced evidence and may require contractual commitments, technical controls, or usage restrictions before approval.
Vendor owners shall monitor material vendor changes, new features, model/provider changes, and incidents that alter the approved risk posture.
The enterprise shall reserve the right to suspend or terminate use of vendor AI services that no longer meet privacy, security, contractual, or governance requirements.

sections.heading: Roles and Responsibilities
bullets: Procurement or Vendor Risk coordinates vendor intake, evidence collection, and renewal review.
Business sponsors define intended use and remain accountable for approved vendor deployment.
Security, Privacy, and Legal review vendor commitments, gaps, incidents, and contractual protections.
Vendor owners monitor material service changes, new features, and risk posture over the relationship lifecycle.

sections.heading: GOVSECURE  |  GOVERNANCE WORKFLOW AND ADD-ON TOOL
paragraphs: Structured generator and policy assembly workbook for producing a right-sized, client-ready AI acceptable use policy for SMB environments.
Use this as a controlled working document. Complete the decision sections first, then issue the policy section as the official employee-facing standard.
tables: Purpose  This generator helps the organization produce a clean, enforceable AI acceptable use policy by capturing company-specific decisions, selecting the right policy clauses, and assembling them into a final client-facing policy.

sections.heading: 1. Generator Overview
paragraphs: This tool is built for small and mid-sized businesses that need a serious policy without writing one from scratch every time. It works like a controlled assembly line: you capture business facts, choose governance decisions, select optional clauses, and issue a clean AI acceptable use policy.
tables: Converts policy choices into a usable AI acceptable use policy.
AI Governance Lead, Security, HR, Legal, or IT owner.
A client-ready policy plus a record of policy decisions.
SMB rollout, policy refresh, audit prep, or managed service packaging.

sections.heading: 2. How to Use This Generator
paragraphs: • Complete the organization profile and policy input pages before editing the policy language.
• Use the policy decision worksheet to define approved tools, prohibited data, human review rules, reporting expectations, and exception handling.
• Select optional clauses only where they match the organization's actual operating model. Do not bolt on rules just to look sophisticated.
• Issue the final policy section as the employee-facing document and retain the generator workbook as internal evidence.

sections.heading: 3. Core Policy Modules Included
tables: Stops ambiguity about who and what the policy covers.
Covered population, in-scope tools, permitted activities.
Prevents confidential or regulated data from leaking into unsafe tools.
Prohibited data classes, uploads, retention, public tool restrictions.
Prevents blind reliance on AI outputs.
Review checkpoints, approval needs, high-impact decisions.
Incident reporting, misuse handling, exception approval, disciplinary path.

sections.heading: 4. Organization Profile and Policy Inputs
paragraphs: Complete this page before finalizing policy text. These inputs drive the level of restriction, scope, and escalation language in the policy.

sections.heading: 5. Policy Decision Worksheet
paragraphs: Choose the stance that matches the organization. This worksheet becomes the decision record behind the issued policy.
tables: Use only named enterprise or approved tools for business work.
Block sensitive data from public or free-tier tools.
Prohibit confidential, regulated, trade secret, source code, customer, or employee data in unapproved tools.
Require human review before relying on AI output for material decisions, external communications, or regulated work.
Escalate chatbots, recommendations, pricing, eligibility, or decision-support that affect customers.
Allow AI-assisted coding only on approved platforms and with secure review and repository controls.
Review new AI features inside existing SaaS platforms before enablement.
Report misuse, leakage, suspicious outputs, and vendor issues immediately to the named channel.
Allow exceptions only with written approval from the governance owner and supporting functions.
State that misuse can result in access restriction, remediation, or disciplinary action.

sections.heading: 6. Optional Clause Library
paragraphs: Select only the modules the client actually needs. Optional clauses should make the policy sharper, not heavier.
tables: Use when customer-facing generative AI or response drafting is allowed.
Add supervision, disclosure, and escalation requirements.
Use when teams generate copy, images, or campaign drafts with AI.
Emphasize brand review, rights checks, and accuracy review.
Use when developers use AI coding assistants or code-generation tools.
Require secure review, secrets protection, and repository controls.
Use when AI supports candidate screening, role descriptions, or people decisions.
Keep strong human review; escalate for high-impact decisions.
Use when the business touches health, financial, export-controlled, or other regulated data.
Keep the restriction language crisp and non-negotiable.
Use when transcription, note generation, or meeting intelligence tools are allowed.
Require participant notice and controlled storage.
Use when existing SaaS tools frequently roll out AI features by default.
State that new AI features require review before activation.
Drafting note  Delete instructions and unchecked references before issuing the final policy. The employee-facing version should read like one coherent standard.

sections.heading: 7. Generated AI Acceptable Use Policy Template
paragraphs: This section is intended to be copied, finalized, and issued as the formal employee-facing policy once the decision worksheets are complete.

sections.heading: {{COMPANY_NAME}} AI Acceptable Use Policy
paragraphs: Effective Date: {{EFFECTIVE_DATE}}   |   Policy Owner: {{DOCUMENT_OWNER}}

sections.heading: 1. Purpose
paragraphs: This policy establishes the rules for acceptable business use of artificial intelligence tools, including generative AI systems, AI-enabled software features, assistants, and automations. Its purpose is to enable responsible use while protecting company, customer, employee, and third-party information from misuse, leakage, inaccuracy, legal exposure, or unmanaged operational risk.

sections.heading: 2. Scope
paragraphs: This policy applies to {{COVERED_POPULATION}} when using AI in connection with company work, company data, company systems, customer interactions, or business decision-making. It covers both standalone AI tools and AI features embedded inside software already used by the organization.

sections.heading: 3. Approved Use
paragraphs: Personnel may use only {{APPROVED_AI_TOOLS}} for business purposes unless a written exception is approved. Acceptable use includes low-risk drafting, summarization, brainstorming, translation, approved automation support, and other uses documented in the company's AI governance process.

sections.heading: 4. Prohibited Use
paragraphs: Personnel must not enter, upload, connect, or expose {{PROHIBITED_DATA_TYPES}} to unapproved AI tools or services. Personnel must not use AI to make final decisions on behalf of the company in high-impact areas without required human review, and must not use AI in ways that violate law, contract, confidentiality obligations, intellectual property rules, employment obligations, or company policy.

sections.heading: 5. Data Handling and Prompt Rules
paragraphs: Before using AI, users must consider what data is being submitted, where the tool processes that data, whether the tool is approved for that data type, and whether retention or model-training settings are acceptable. Sensitive, regulated, confidential, customer, employee, source code, financial, legal, or trade secret information may only be used where expressly approved by the organization and supported by technical and contractual controls.

sections.heading: 6. Output Review and Human Accountability
paragraphs: Users remain responsible for the accuracy, appropriateness, legality, and business impact of work produced with AI assistance. AI output must be reviewed before it is acted upon, shared externally, relied upon for material business decisions, or incorporated into customer, employee, legal, financial, security, or compliance-sensitive activity. AI does not replace human judgment, approval, or accountability.

sections.heading: 7. Customer-Facing or External Use
paragraphs: Any chatbot, automated response, recommendation, decision-support, public content generation, or customer-facing AI workflow must follow {{CUSTOMER_FACING_RULE}} and any additional review requirements set by the company. Where required, human escalation paths, disclosures, or quality controls must be implemented before launch.

sections.heading: 8. AI Features in Third-Party Software
paragraphs: New AI features inside existing SaaS or business applications must not be enabled or used for company work until the organization confirms their data handling, security posture, contractual terms, and approved use conditions. Vendor claims are not enough; review comes first.

sections.heading: 9. Incident Reporting and Exceptions
paragraphs: Users must promptly report suspected misuse, data leakage, hallucinations with material impact, unsafe outputs, unauthorized AI tool usage, or vendor AI concerns to {{REPORTING_CHANNEL}}. Exceptions to this policy may be granted only through written approval by {{APPROVAL_CONTACT}} and must be documented.

sections.heading: 10. Violations
paragraphs: Violations of this policy may result in access restriction, corrective coaching, remediation requirements, disciplinary action, vendor disablement, or other response measures based on the nature and severity of the issue.

sections.heading: 11. Review and Maintenance
paragraphs: This policy will be reviewed periodically and updated when the company's AI tooling, risk exposure, legal environment, or governance program materially changes.

sections.heading: 9. Quick Implementation Checklist
paragraphs: • Confirm the list of approved AI platforms before issuing the policy.
• Validate prohibited data language against real business data classes and contractual obligations.
• Make sure reporting channels, approvers, and exception contacts are real people and active workflows.
• Pair the policy with awareness training, a use case intake process, and a registry so the policy has somewhere to live beyond a PDF graveyard.
• Review the policy whenever a major AI platform, customer-facing use case, or regulated data workflow changes.

paragraphs: The most important AI vendor question is often simple: “Will you use our data to train your models?”
Treat “embedded AI features” in existing tools as new risk, not “already approved because we bought the platform.”
Approval conditions are powerful—use them (pilot-only, no PII, restricted users, logging mandatory).

paragraphs:

sections.heading: Recommended Review Frequency by Tier
paragraphs: Low: Annual
Moderate: Semi-Annual
High: Quarterly
Critical: Continuous monitoring + Quarterly formal revalidation

sections.heading: Quick Tips
paragraphs: Monitoring is where governance becomes real. Intake gets applause; monitoring prevents headlines.
Trigger-based revalidation is just as important as calendar-based reviews.
If the vendor changed the model and nobody reviewed the impact, your “approval” is stale.

sections.heading: AI Incident Response Checklist
paragraphs: A practical checklist for triaging, containing, investigating, remediating, and closing incidents involving AI tools, models, agents, data exposure, unsafe outputs, or unauthorized AI use.
tables:
Purpose
Use this checklist during an active AI-related incident to make sure the response team covers the basics without missing the ugly details: what happened, what data or outputs were affected, how to contain harm, which stakeholders must be notified, and how to close the incident with evidence and lessons learned.
At least annually and after major incidents
AI Acceptable Use Policy / AI Use Case Registry
Security, IT, privacy, legal, business owners, incident managers

sections.heading: How to use this checklist
bullets: Use it during a live incident or tabletop, not as post-incident fan fiction.
If a step is not applicable, mark why; if it is incomplete, name the owner and due date.
Log material facts, decisions, and evidence references while the incident is happening.
Any confirmed incident should also update the AI Use Case Registry, shadow AI log, and relevant risk or vendor records where applicable.

sections.heading: 1. Identify and validate the incident
paragraphs: Confirm that the event is real enough to open an incident and describe what makes it AI-related.
tables: Capture the initial report and time of discovery.
Record who reported it, when it was detected, how it was observed, and whether this is active, suspected, or historical.
Examples include prompt leakage, unauthorized AI use, harmful output, model misuse, agent overreach, vendor outage, data exposure, or policy breach.
Identify the impacted tool, model, workflow, or vendor.
List the AI system, feature, API, embedded product, or shadow AI tool involved.
Confirm whether the issue meets incident criteria.
Determine whether the event requires formal response versus routine bug handling, quality tuning, or policy coaching.

sections.heading: 2. Classify severity and activate the response team
paragraphs: Decide how serious the issue is and who needs to be pulled in before the incident grows legs.
tables: Assign a provisional severity or priority.
Use your incident scale based on business impact, data sensitivity, external exposure, operational disruption, or safety implications.
Identify incident lead and core responders.
Name the coordinator plus security, IT, privacy, legal, vendor management, or business owners as needed.
Decide whether a war room or formal incident bridge is required.
Use this for high-severity cases, external exposure, or time-sensitive containment decisions.
Record the first executive notification trigger if needed.
Note whether leadership must be informed immediately based on severity, customer impact, or legal risk.

sections.heading: 3. Contain the immediate risk
paragraphs: The first job is to stop further harm without casually smashing evidence or business continuity in the process.
tables: Decide whether to disable or restrict the AI capability.
Pause accounts, APIs, plugins, agents, automations, or routing where continued use could worsen harm.
Block unsafe data flows or integrations.
Suspend file uploads, connectors, token access, shared mailboxes, knowledge sources, or system-to-system actions if necessary.
Define manual workarounds, human review steps, or service degradations so the business can keep breathing.
Capture who authorized containment, when it happened, and what systems or users were affected.

Containment note
If the incident involves autonomous actions, outbound integrations, or customer-facing responses, prioritize the fastest safe kill switch available. A graceful shutdown is lovely. A preventable blast radius is not.

sections.heading: 4. Preserve evidence and establish facts
paragraphs: AI incidents get muddy fast. Evidence discipline matters because memory is a terrible log source.
tables: Preserve relevant logs, prompts, outputs, tickets, and screenshots.
Capture records from AI platforms, identity systems, applications, proxies, chat histories, audits, or SIEM sources.
Identify the data and users affected.
Document what information was entered, exposed, processed, or output, and which users, customers, or employees were impacted.
Record the incident timeline as facts emerge.
Track first observed time, last known good state, containment milestones, and key decisions.
Note uncertainty and known gaps honestly.
Mark what is confirmed, what is suspected, and what still needs verification.

sections.heading: 5. Assess impact, exposure, and downstream harm
paragraphs: Move beyond the technical event and assess what actually matters to the organization and affected people.
tables: Determine confidentiality, integrity, or availability impact.
Assess whether data leaked, outputs were manipulated, systems acted incorrectly, or services became unavailable.
Determine whether the incident influenced decisions or actions.
Check whether people or systems acted on harmful outputs, recommendations, classifications, or generated content.
Identify customer, employee, regulatory, or reputational impact.
Call out public exposure, HR issues, legal exposure, financial consequences, or trust damage.
Decide whether the issue involves a high-consequence context.
Flag incidents affecting HR, finance, healthcare, safety, legal, or customer-facing decision processes.

sections.heading: 6. Escalate and communicate
paragraphs: Get the right people involved before communication becomes improvisational theater.
tables: Engage privacy, legal, compliance, and executive stakeholders where triggers apply.
Use established criteria for personal data, contractual exposure, public communication risk, or regulated obligations.
Assess whether vendor escalation is required.
Notify the AI vendor or SaaS provider when the incident involves their platform, model, outage, or shared responsibility.
Provide a controlled message for help desk, managers, or employees if user action or temporary restrictions are needed.
Consider customers, partners, insurers, regulators, or law enforcement if the facts and thresholds support it.

Notification note
Do not guess on breach, notice, or regulatory thresholds in the heat of the incident. Escalate to privacy and legal early when personal data, customer harm, or contractual commitments may be involved.

sections.heading: 7. Eradicate the root cause or unsafe condition
paragraphs: Containment buys time; eradication is where you remove the cause, not just the symptom wearing a fake moustache.
tables: Identify the likely root cause or failure mode.
Examples include weak access control, bad configuration, risky vendor behavior, shadow AI use, poor prompts, absent guardrails, or broken oversight.
Implement corrective technical or process changes.
Rotate keys, disable plugins, patch workflows, tighten access, add filtering, revise prompts, or change integrations as needed.
Remove or remediate unauthorized AI usage.
Address shadow AI tools, unapproved accounts, or workflows that bypassed governance requirements.
Confirm whether additional validation is needed before re-enabling service.
Define what evidence, testing, or approvals must exist before rollback or go-live.

sections.heading: 8. Recover safely and monitor
paragraphs: Recovery is not just turning it back on and hoping the ghost has left the machine.
tables: Approve the recovery plan and scope.
Document what will be restored, for whom, under what restrictions, and with whose approval.
Test the restored capability or workaround.
Check whether controls now work as intended and whether outputs, access, and data flows behave safely.
Increase monitoring for recurrence or residual risk.
Add focused logging, alerting, sample review, business-owner checks, or vendor follow-up during the watch period.
Document business acceptance of residual risk if any remains.
Record any temporary exception, monitoring period, or compensating control that supports recovery.

sections.heading: 9. Capture lessons learned and governance updates
paragraphs: A closed incident that teaches nothing is basically tuition without the degree.
tables: Identify what detection, policy, training, vendor control, or architecture weakness allowed the incident to happen or linger.
Update policies, standards, or procedures where needed.
Adjust acceptable use rules, incident playbooks, procurement checks, data handling rules, or review gates.
Feed findings into training and awareness.
Decide what employees, admins, developers, or managers need to be told or retrained on.
Update related risk, vendor, or use-case records.
Tie the incident to the right use case, vendor file, and governance records so the program actually learns.

sections.heading: 10. Closure, lessons learned, and sign-off
paragraphs: Use this final checkpoint before the incident is formally closed, logged, and handed back into business-as-usual governance.
tables: The final incident summary is complete.
The record includes timeline, root cause hypothesis or finding, impact summary, actions taken, and open residual risks.
Required notifications and communications have been completed or deferred with approval.
Capture internal notices, customer notices, regulator decisions, vendor notices, or law enforcement engagement where relevant.
Corrective actions and owners have been assigned.
Document what changes are required to policy, training, technical controls, vendors, or monitoring, and who owns them.
Related governance records have been updated.
Update the AI Use Case Registry, shadow AI log, vendor file, incident tracker, and control library as needed.
Mark whether the incident is closed, monitoring-only, or remains open due to pending remediation.

GS-CHKL-INTAKEFO-09.title: AI Use Case Intake Form Checklist (Template)

sections.heading: AI Use Case Intake Form Checklist (Template)
paragraphs: Purpose: Use this checklist to ensure all required information is collected before AI use-case review, risk triage, and approval.

sections.heading: 1) Business Purpose & Scope
paragraphs:
☐ Clear use case title provided
☐ Business problem/opportunity described
☐ Intended outcome / success criteria defined
☐ Scope defined (pilot / limited / enterprise-wide)
☐ In-scope users identified (employees/customers/vendors)
☐ Out-of-scope use explicitly stated
☐ Whether use case is internal-only or customer-facing identified

sections.heading: 2) AI System Description
paragraphs:
☐ AI type identified (Generative / Predictive / Classification / Agent / Other)
☐ Tool/vendor/platform name provided
☐ Model source identified (internal model / vendor API / SaaS feature / open-source model)
☐ Deployment model identified (SaaS / API / on-prem / cloud-hosted)
☐ System architecture summary provided (high level)
☐ Integrations listed (apps, APIs, databases, connectors)
☐ Whether AI can take actions (write/update/send/delete) identified

sections.heading: 3) Data & Privacy Information
paragraphs:
☐ Data types listed (public / internal / confidential / regulated)
☐ Personal data involved? (PII) — Yes / No
☐ Sensitive data involved? (PHI/PCI/financial/minors/etc.) — Yes / No
☐ Data sources identified (systems, files, third parties)
☐ Cross-border data transfer expected? — Yes / No
☐ Data retention/storage behavior documented
☐ Vendor data usage terms reviewed (training/retention/telemetry)
☐ Data minimization considered
☐ Privacy notice / consent implications identified (if applicable)

sections.heading: 4) Decision Impact & Human Oversight
paragraphs:
☐ AI output purpose identified (advisory / assistive / automated decision support / full automation)
☐ Whether outputs influence decisions about people identified
☐ Human review required before action? — Yes / No
☐ Human reviewer role identified
☐ Escalation path for bad outputs defined
☐ Fallback process documented if AI is unavailable or incorrect

sections.heading: 5) Risk & Compliance Screening
paragraphs:
☐ Potential regulatory impact identified (privacy, sector-specific, AI, cybersecurity, consumer protection, etc.)
☐ Internal policy impact identified (AI policy, acceptable use, security policy, data handling policy)
☐ Third-party risk review required? — Yes / No
☐ Contract/DPA review required? — Yes / No
☐ Records retention / audit logging requirement identified
☐ Need for DPIA/PIA identified (if personal/sensitive data)
☐ Need for legal review identified
☐ Need for security architecture review identified

sections.heading: 6) Security Controls (Initial Intake Check)
paragraphs:
☐ Authentication method identified (SSO/MFA/local account)
☐ Access roles/users defined
☐ Least privilege considered
☐ Logging/audit trail availability confirmed
☐ Data encryption in transit/at rest confirmed (if known)
☐ API keys/secrets handling method identified
☐ Prompt/input restrictions considered (sensitive data rules)
☐ Output handling/storage location identified
☐ Incident reporting contact identified

sections.heading: 7) Vendor / Third-Party AI Questions (if applicable)
paragraphs:
☐ Vendor name and product identified
☐ Hosting region / data residency stated
☐ Subprocessors disclosed
☐ Vendor states whether customer data is used for model training
☐ Security/compliance documentation available (SOC 2, ISO 27001, etc.)
☐ Incident notification commitments reviewed
☐ Right-to-audit / assessment rights identified (if enterprise contract)
☐ SLA / uptime dependency understood

sections.heading: 8) Testing, Monitoring & Operations
paragraphs:
☐ Success metrics defined (accuracy, cycle time, cost, quality, user satisfaction, etc.)
☐ Test plan defined before production use
☐ Known limitations / failure modes documented
☐ Monitoring owner assigned
☐ Review frequency proposed (monthly/quarterly/etc.)
☐ Drift/hallucination/error monitoring considered (if relevant)
☐ Change management process for model/vendor updates identified

sections.heading: 9) Classification & Triage Outcome (to be completed by governance team)
paragraphs:
☐ Intake complete
☐ Additional information required
☐ Low Risk
☐ Moderate Risk
☐ High Risk
☐ Critical Risk

Required Next Steps (check all that apply):
☐ Basic Risk Assessment
☐ Security Review
☐ Privacy Review / DPIA
☐ Legal Review
☐ Vendor Risk Assessment
☐ Threat Model
☐ Model Card / Documentation
☐ Evaluation Plan
☐ Monitoring Plan
☐ Human Oversight Plan
☐ Executive Approval

sections.heading: Quick Tips (so this form doesn’t become “checkbox theater”)
paragraphs: Require evidence links where possible (architecture diagram, vendor docs, DPA, test plan).
Reject “TBD” for data types, integrations, and human oversight.
If the AI can write/update/delete/send, escalate automatically.
If regulated data is involved, route to privacy/security/legal before pilot.
If you want, I can also turn this into a fillable version in:

sections.heading: Word form format
paragraphs: ServiceNow/Jira intake workflow fields (with conditional logic)

GS-CHKL-OTHERTEM-14.title: Other Vital AI Governance Checklist Templates:

sections.heading: Other Vital AI Governance Checklist Templates:
paragraphs:
AI Inventory & System Registry Checklist
Tracks every AI use case/model/vendor, owner, purpose, risk tier, environment, and status.
(If it’s not in the inventory, it shouldn’t be in production.)

AI Policy-to-Control Mapping Checklist
Ensures each AI policy requirement is mapped to actual controls, owners, and evidence.
Great for avoiding “policy theater.”

AI Third-Party / Vendor Due Diligence Checklist
For vendor AI tools, SaaS features, APIs, LLM providers, subprocessors, data retention/training terms, incident commitments, audit docs.

AI Security Review Checklist
Focused on technical security controls: access, MFA/SSO, API scopes, logging, secrets, DLP, network exposure, prompt injection protections.

AI Privacy & DPIA Screening Checklist
Fast triage to decide when a DPIA/PIA is required and what privacy reviews are triggered (PII, sensitive data, transfers, consent, notices).

AI Model Validation / Testing Checklist
Covers test plan, quality metrics, edge cases, hallucination checks, failure scenarios, bias/fairness checks, and go-live readiness.

AI Monitoring & Revalidation Checklist
Ensures post-launch controls exist: drift/performance checks, logging review, incident review, retraining/update review, periodic reapproval.

AI Change Management Checklist
Used when model versions, prompts, vendors, integrations, or data sources change.
Prevents “silent changes” from bypassing governance.

AI Human Oversight & Escalation Checklist
Defines reviewer roles, decision boundaries, escalation path, override rules, fallback process, and accountability for bad outputs.

AI Incident Response Checklist (AI-Specific)
For hallucinations, harmful outputs, data leakage, prompt injection, model abuse, unauthorized use, vendor outages, or model behavior drift.

AI Training & Role-Based Awareness Checklist
Ensures business users, developers, reviewers, and executives get the right training (not the same generic slide deck for everyone).

AI Regulatory Change Impact Assessment Checklist
For reviewing new laws/regulations and translating them into impacted policies, controls, procedures, evidence requirements, and deadlines.

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: GOVSECURE

sections.heading: AI Procurement Review Checklist
paragraphs: A practical, client-ready checklist for evaluating AI tools, AI-enabled vendors, and AI procurement decisions before purchase, renewal, or expansion.
tables:
Purpose
Use this checklist to confirm that business need, security, privacy, legal, procurement, operational, and AI-specific governance requirements have been reviewed before an AI product is purchased, renewed, materially changed, or broadly deployed.
At least annually or upon major AI procurement change
Procurement, IT, Security, Privacy, Legal, Business Owner
AI tools, AI-enabled SaaS, copilots, model APIs, embedded AI features
New purchase, renewal, upgrade, high-risk change

sections.heading: How to use this checklist
bullets: Use it before a new AI vendor is purchased, before an existing vendor adds material AI features, or before an AI contract is renewed.
Complete the sections in order and capture evidence in the Notes / evidence column.
Escalate any item that cannot be confirmed, any answer that introduces high-risk data or decisions, or any vendor that refuses to provide reasonable assurance evidence.
This checklist supports procurement review; it does not replace detailed security, privacy, legal, or architecture review where required.

sections.heading: 1. Initiate the procurement review
paragraphs: Confirm that the AI purchase is in scope and that the request has a named business owner, a real need, and a review trigger.
tables: Identify whether the review is for a new purchase, renewal, expansion, pilot, or material feature change.
Request ticket, business memo, or intake link.
Assign a business owner and reviewer set.
Name the business sponsor plus procurement, IT, security, privacy, legal, and operations contacts as needed.
Describe the business problem to be solved.
State the operational need, why AI is being considered, and what success should look like.
Check for duplicate or already-approved tools.
Confirm whether an approved platform already provides equivalent capability.
Define when procurement needs a go / no-go decision and what must be reviewed before then.

sections.heading: 2. Confirm the business case and sponsorship
paragraphs: Make sure the purchase has a clear sponsor, defined value, intended users, and a realistic rollout plan.
tables: Capture the expected benefit such as productivity, quality, speed, revenue enablement, customer support, or risk reduction.
Business case, ROI estimate, or expected KPI.
List which teams, roles, or geographies will use the tool and whether usage is optional or required.
Clarify whether the use is internal or external.
Confirm whether outputs are only for internal support or whether customers, partners, or applicants may be affected.
Confirm whether the tool influences hiring, finance, legal, customer decisions, safety, or regulated activities.
Verify that an accountable leader supports the purchase and accepts ownership of the business outcome.

sections.heading: 3. Profile the vendor, product, and deployment model
paragraphs: Understand exactly what is being bought, how it works, and where it fits in the environment.
tables: Record the full vendor and product name.
Capture the legal vendor name, product name, edition, and licensing model.
Quote, order form, or vendor profile.
Describe the AI capability being purchased.
State whether the solution uses embedded AI, third-party foundation models, custom models, agent workflows, copilots, or model APIs.
Confirm whether the service is SaaS, API-based, browser-based, desktop, mobile, or on-prem/hybrid.
List connected systems, plug-ins, browser extensions, identity requirements, or data sources the tool will use.
Confirm data residency and service footprint.
Document where the service is hosted and which regions process or store data.

sections.heading: 4. Review data handling and privacy expectations
paragraphs: AI purchases often fail here. Confirm what data goes in, what data comes out, and what the vendor is allowed to do with it.
tables: Identify whether prompts or datasets may contain public, internal, confidential, personal, regulated, or customer data.
Data profile or sample use cases.
Check for prohibited or restricted data.
Confirm whether highly sensitive data categories are in scope and whether policy allows them in this platform.
Confirm whether customer inputs, outputs, logs, or files are used for model training, fine-tuning, service improvement, or retained beyond business need.
DPA, privacy terms, or vendor FAQ.
Understand how long prompts, uploads, logs, and outputs are retained and how deletion is requested.
Determine whether a DPIA, DPA, SCCs, or other privacy review is required before use.
Privacy review notes and legal requirements.
Common failure point: teams assume 'enterprise' means data will never be retained or used for training. Verify it in writing.

sections.heading: 5. Review security, identity, and access controls
paragraphs: Do not buy a clever AI tool that behaves like an unlocked side door.
tables: Check SSO, MFA, federation, SCIM, role-based access control, and support for managed identities or service accounts.
Review access model and least privilege.
Confirm whether roles can be restricted by function, workspace, project, dataset, or admin capability.
Check encryption in transit and at rest, plus any options for customer-managed keys or private networking where relevant.
Review logging, alerting, and admin visibility.
Confirm whether admins can review usage, monitor access, export logs, and support investigations.
Collect relevant SOC reports, ISO certifications, penetration testing summaries, or equivalent assurance artifacts.

sections.heading: 6. Assess AI-specific governance and model risk
paragraphs: This is the section most vendors hope you skip. Do not grant that wish.
tables: Understand whether the vendor discloses which model families are used, whether model providers may change, and what notice is given for material changes.
Check whether the tool supports human review, confidence indicators, grounded retrieval, policy controls, or other mechanisms that reduce harmful output use.
Confirm whether the product only assists humans or whether it can trigger actions, decisions, communications, or workflow execution automatically.
Check for bias, fairness, or sensitive-use concerns.
Determine whether the tool could create discriminatory or inappropriate outcomes in HR, finance, legal, healthcare, education, or other sensitive contexts.
State what human review is required before outputs are relied upon, especially for customer-facing or high-impact decisions.
Escalation rule: if the tool influences high-impact decisions or automates actions, require explicit human oversight and formal sign-off.

sections.heading: 7. Review commercial, legal, and contractual terms
paragraphs: Procurement should not sign a contract that quietly gives away your data, leverage, or exit options.
tables: Understand license metrics, overage risk, API usage pricing, pilot conversion terms, and renewal behavior.
Confirm security and privacy commitments in contract.
Make sure commercial terms align with the security and privacy promises made during review.
Check service levels and support obligations.
Review uptime commitments, response targets, support model, and escalation paths.
Assess incident notification and cooperation terms.
Confirm whether the contract requires timely incident notice, investigation support, and remediation obligations.
Review termination, data return, and deletion rights.
Check what happens at exit, including data portability, deletion, and dependency unwind.

sections.heading: 8. Confirm implementation readiness and operating controls
paragraphs: A tool is not ready because the sales demo sparkled. Confirm it can actually be governed in production.
tables: Identify implementation owner and rollout path.
Define who will configure, test, deploy, and support the solution after purchase.
List conditions such as approved data boundaries, configured SSO, logging, admin roles, policy training, or restricted pilot scope.
Confirm that employees will receive acceptable use guidance, escalation channels, and any role-based instructions.
Set when the solution must be re-reviewed, such as vendor feature changes, new integrations, sensitive data use, or expanded automation.
Ensure the purchase and associated use cases will be entered into the AI use case registry or vendor inventory.
Minimum go-live expectation: approved identity setup, defined owner, documented safeguards, user guidance, and registry entry.

sections.heading: 9. Finalize the decision and procurement handoff
paragraphs: Close the loop with a clear decision, conditions, and accountable owners.
tables: List open issues, accepted risks, compensating controls, and decisions still pending.
Mark approved, approved with conditions, deferred, pilot only, or rejected.
State what must happen before contract signature, before pilot, and before full production use.
Assign action owners and due dates.
Name who owns each follow-up item, including procurement, security, business, IT, or legal.
Save the completed checklist and supporting evidence in the approved repository.

sections.heading: 10. Approval and control record
paragraphs: Complete this summary after the checklist review is finished and the procurement decision is made.
tables: APPROVED / APPROVED WITH CONDITIONS / DEFERRED / REJECTED

B) "Auto-High" Triggers — tick any that apply fi any n automatically sets tier to HIGH

sections.heading: AI Security Review Checklist
paragraphs: Template • Version 1.0 • 2026-04-03

sections.heading: Purpose
paragraphs: Use this checklist to validate completion of required governance activities and maintain an audit-ready record.

sections.heading: How to Use
paragraphs: Mark each item as completed (☒) when done. Add owner, due date, and evidence link/location for audit traceability.

sections.heading: Architecture & Boundaries
tables: System diagram reviewed (data flows + trust boundaries)
Network exposure controlled (Private Link/VNet/private endpoints where applicable)
Environment separation enforced (dev/test/prod) and secure defaults established

sections.heading: Identity & Access
tables: SSO enforced and MFA for admins
RBAC/ABAC least privilege validated (including service identities)
Access reviews scheduled and ownership defined
Secrets and token flows governed (rotation, short-lived creds where possible)

sections.heading: Data Protection
tables: Sensitive data handling rules defined (prompts/outputs; prohibited data types)
DLP controls applied where applicable (prompts, outputs, connectors)

sections.heading: Logging, Monitoring & Detection
tables: Alerts for abuse/anomalous usage configured (rate limits, spikes, exfil indicators)
RAG protections applied where relevant (scoped indexes, retrieval filtering, permission checks)

sections.heading: AI-Specific Threat Controls
tables: Prompt injection controls (input validation, tool allowlists, output constraints)
Tool poisoning controls (trusted tool registry, signed/approved tools)
Data exfiltration controls for agentic workflows (connector scoping, egress checks)

sections.heading: Approval
tables: Security decision recorded (Approve / Approve with Conditions / Reject)
Remediation actions assigned with owners and due dates

GS-CHKL-SHADOWAI-20.title: GOVSECURE  |  GOVERNANCE WORKFLOW AND ADD-ON TOOL

sections.heading: GOVSECURE  |  GOVERNANCE WORKFLOW AND ADD-ON TOOL
Shadow AI Discovery Workflow
paragraphs: Standard operating workflow for identifying, validating, triaging, and remediating unapproved or unmanaged AI use across the organization.
Use this as a controlled workflow document and pair it with a live findings log or case tracker. Update it whenever discovery methods, review criteria, or escalation paths materially change.
tables: Purpose  This workflow helps the organization find AI use that escaped formal governance, determine what data and risk are involved, decide whether the activity can be allowed, restricted, replaced, or stopped, and document the result in a repeatable way.

sections.heading: 1. Workflow Overview
paragraphs: Shadow AI is the use of AI tools, models, plugins, automations, or vendor features outside the organization’s approved governance process. The issue is not that people are experimenting. The issue is that the business cannot manage what it cannot see.
Common examples of shadow AI
• Employees entering confidential text into consumer AI tools that were never approved for business use.
• A vendor quietly enabling generative AI features that process company or customer data under new terms.
• Browser plugins or meeting assistants that record, summarize, or route internal content without security review.
• Teams using AI automations connected to email, CRM, HR, finance, or source code systems outside approved controls.
tables: Unapproved AI tools, unmanaged AI features inside existing vendors, risky employee usage patterns, and workarounds that bypass standard review.
Reduces data leakage, weak contracting, unreviewed automation, and surprise customer or employee impact.
AI Governance Lead with Security and IT support.
Browser extensions, public AI chat tools, SaaS add-ons, expense claims, user tips, vendor rollouts, or incident leads.

sections.heading: 2. Discovery Sources and Roles
paragraphs: A good discovery program does not rely on one magic source. It uses several lightweight signals that, together, reveal where AI use has drifted outside policy.
Recommended discovery sources
Roles and responsibilities
tables: Extensions, downloads, bookmarks, desktop apps, or repeated access to public AI tools.
Connected apps, OAuth grants, new feature enablement, shadow sign-ups.
Vendor AI features and unreviewed app usage.
AI subscriptions, trials, or team-paid tools.
Paid AI tooling outside formal approval.
Manager tips, user questions, and self-disclosures.
Privacy, security, legal, or compliance observations.
Owns workflow, coordinates review, maintains findings log, and drives closure.
Reviews data exposure, technical risks, access, logging, and containment needs.
Validates approved alternatives, tenant controls, and support options.
Reviews data handling, contract terms, privacy impact, and legal triggers when needed.
Explains the business purpose, confirms users and data, and owns remediation actions.

sections.heading: 3. Standard Workflow Steps
paragraphs: The workflow below is designed to be fast enough for SMB use and serious enough to stand up to audit or leadership scrutiny.
Operating rule:
A shadow AI finding is not complete when the team says “we stopped using it.” It is complete when the organization has evidence of the decision, the remediation, and the closure.
tables: Capture the source of concern, such as a browser signal, vendor feature, expense, manager report, or incident note.
Confirm the exact tool, model, plugin, or AI-enabled feature in use. Do not investigate ghosts.
Identify the team, owner, purpose, users, integrations, and business dependency.
Determine what data is entered, generated, stored, shared, or used for model training.
Assign an initial risk rating using data sensitivity, user impact, automation level, and vendor concerns.
Allow, allow with conditions, replace, restrict, block, or escalate. Document why.
Issue instructions to users, managers, IT, procurement, or security. Capture deadlines.
Verify action completion, update the log, and record the final status in the AI use case registry where applicable.

sections.heading: 4. Risk and Disposition Guidance
paragraphs: Not every finding deserves the same reaction. Some cases can be brought under governance quickly. Some need to be shut down before they become tomorrow’s apology email.
tables: Non-sensitive drafting or summarization with no system integration and no customer impact.
Move to approved tool or log the use case and remind the team of policy conditions.
Internal confidential data, recurring team use, or unmanaged vendor AI features inside existing systems.
Require approved tenant, disable training on customer data, set usage guardrails, and document owner.
Sensitive data, customer content, HR / finance impact, automation, or unclear vendor terms.
Pause expansion, involve security and privacy, and define controls before use can continue.
Regulated data, major decision impact, legal or contractual conflict, or active unsafe behavior.
Contain access, preserve evidence, notify stakeholders, and route for executive decision if needed.

sections.heading: 5. Discovery Investigation Checklist
paragraphs: Use the questions below for each finding. The goal is not paperwork for its own sake. The goal is to avoid guessing.
tables: What tool, model, plugin, or AI feature was identified?
Which team or individuals are using it?
What business purpose does it serve?
What data is entered, uploaded, recorded, or generated?
Is any confidential, personal, regulated, or customer data involved?
Does the tool integrate with email, files, CRM, HR, finance, code, or other systems?
Does the vendor retain data or train models on submitted content?
Is there an approved alternative already available?
What immediate action is required: allow, restrict, replace, block, or escalate?
Who owns remediation and by when?

sections.heading: 6. Shadow AI Findings Log Template
paragraphs: Use this log as the minimum operating record for open findings. If your team manages findings in a ticketing system, keep the same fields.
tables: Minimum standard: Every finding should link to a named owner, a disposition, and a due date. A log without ownership is just a museum of avoided decisions.

sections.heading: 7. Example Findings and Actions
paragraphs: These examples show how the workflow can be used in real SMB situations without drowning the team in process.
tables: Public AI chatbot used to draft internal marketing copy
Move team to approved enterprise tool and record the use case.
Meeting assistant records customer calls under free trial terms
Pause use until retention, consent, and contract terms are reviewed.
Sensitive employee data and unmanaged extension risk require containment and removal.
CRM vendor turned on gen-AI drafting feature by default
Confirm data-use settings, logging, and user guidance before rollout.

sections.heading: 8. Approval and Control Record
paragraphs: Use this section to document approval of the workflow itself and the individual accountable for operating it.
tables: At least annually or upon material change
AI Use Case Registry; AI Acceptable Use Policy; Vendor Risk Review

sections.heading: Page 3

Page 5
paragraphs:
GovSecure | AI Governance Template Pack
This policy applies to employees, contractors, consultants, business units, and third parties using AI tools,
AI-enabled features, or AI outputs on behalf of {{COMPANY_NAME}}.
Client customization note: {{2_CLIENT_NOTES}}
3. Governance objectives
The objectives of this policy are to: (a) define accountability, (b) require use-case intake and review, (c)
maintain a central registry, (d) review third-party AI risk, and (e) support safe, business-aligned adoption.
Client customization note: {{3_CLIENT_NOTES}}
4. Roles and responsibilities
{{EXEC_SPONSOR}} serves as executive sponsor. {{AI_GOVERNANCE_LEAD}} coordinates the operating
process. Business owners remain accountable for the purpose, outcomes, and ongoing suitability of
approved use cases.
Client customization note: {{4_CLIENT_NOTES}}
5. Use-case intake and review
All new or materially changed AI use cases must be submitted through the approved intake process before
launch, unless expressly exempted in writing by {{APPROVING_AUTHORITY}}. Use cases will be rated
{{RISK_SCALE}} and escalated when they involve sensitive data, customer impact, employment decisions,
legal or financial outcomes, or significant automation.
Client customization note: {{5_CLIENT_NOTES}}
6. Registry requirement
All submitted, approved, rejected, paused, and retired AI use cases must be recorded in the AI Use Case
Registry with an owner, risk level, approval status, review date, and required controls.
Client customization note: {{6_CLIENT_NOTES}}
7. Vendor due diligence
AI vendors and AI-enabled service providers must undergo a right-sized review covering security, privacy,
data handling, contractual protections, retention, subprocessors, and incident response before purchase or
production use.
Client customization note: {{7_CLIENT_NOTES}}
8. Human oversight
High-impact or externally facing AI outputs must be reviewed by an accountable human prior to use in
decisions that could materially affect customers, employees, finances, contracts, compliance, or safety.
Client customization note: {{8_CLIENT_NOTES}}
9. Monitoring and revalidation
Approved use cases must be re-evaluated when the purpose, data used, vendor, model, user population, or
downstream decision impact changes, or when an incident, complaint, or control failure occurs.
Client customization note: {{9_CLIENT_NOTES}}
10. Exceptions and violations
Any exception to this policy requires documented approval. Misuse of AI tools, bypassing review, or entering
restricted data into unapproved tools may result in remediation, tool restrictions, disciplinary action, or other
corrective measures.
Client-ready editable template pack

sections.heading: Page 6
paragraphs:
GovSecure | AI Governance Template Pack
Client customization note: {{10_CLIENT_NOTES}}
Template 4 - AI Acceptable Use Policy Template
Use case: This template is intended for employee-facing communication. Keep it plain, direct, and
enforceable. Fancy words are optional; clarity is not.
Document control
Audience {{AUP_AUDIENCE}}
Policy owner {{AUP_OWNER}}
Effective date {{AUP_EFFECTIVE_DATE}}
Questions / reporting channel {{AUP_CONTACT_CHANNEL}}
Permitted use
Employees may use approved AI tools for low- and medium-risk business activities such as drafting,
summarization, brainstorming, knowledge assistance, or productivity support, subject to this policy and all
applicable company policies.
Prohibited data
Employees must not enter {{RESTRICTED_DATA_TYPES}} into unapproved AI tools or any AI tool where
contractual, technical, or policy controls do not permit such data handling.
Output review
AI outputs must be reviewed by the user for accuracy, bias, confidentiality, appropriateness, and business fit
before being shared or relied upon.
Restricted decisions
Employees may not use AI outputs as the sole basis for decisions involving hiring, termination, legal advice,
regulated obligations, customer rights, pricing, credit, safety, or any other significant impact area without
approved oversight controls.
Shadow AI
Employees must not procure, connect, or use AI tools for company work outside approved channels without
disclosure and review.
Incident reporting
Suspected data exposure, harmful output, unauthorized use, or other AI-related issues must be reported
immediately through {{AUP_CONTACT_CHANNEL}}.
Consequences
Failure to follow this policy may result in access restriction, remediation requirements, disciplinary action, or
other measures consistent with company policy.
