GS-CHKL-THIRDPAR-22.title: AI Third-Party / Vendor Due Diligence Checklist

sections.heading: AI Third-Party / Vendor Due Diligence Checklist
paragraphs: Template • Version 1.0 • 2026-04-03

sections.heading: Purpose
paragraphs: Use this checklist to validate completion of required governance activities and maintain an audit-ready record.

sections.heading: How to Use
paragraphs: Mark each item as completed (☒) when done. Add owner, due date, and evidence link/location for audit traceability.

sections.heading: Vendor Basics
tables: Vendor legal entity and product/service scope confirmed
Deployment model and hosting region(s)/data residency documented

sections.heading: AI-Specific Questions
tables: Is customer data used for training? confirmed (and opt-out/no-training configuration documented)
Model/version change notification process documented (release notes; material changes)
Audit logging and export capability confirmed

sections.heading: Security Controls
tables: Encryption in transit and at rest confirmed
Security documentation collected (SOC 2/ISO/pen test summary/security whitepaper)
Incident notification commitments and timelines confirmed

sections.heading: Privacy & Compliance
tables: DPA reviewed; retention and deletion terms confirmed
Data minimization supported; data export/exit supported

sections.heading: Decision & Conditions
tables: Approval decision recorded (Approve / Approve with Conditions / Defer / Reject)
Approval conditions documented (e.g., pilot-only, no PII, logging required)
Re-review cadence and triggers defined (renewal, new features, incidents, model changes)

sections.heading: AI Tool Inventory Checklist
paragraphs: A practical checklist for identifying, validating, documenting, and maintaining visibility over AI tools used or proposed across the organization.
tables:
Purpose
Use this checklist to make sure the organization has a disciplined process for discovering AI tools, recording ownership, assessing data exposure, flagging shadow AI, and keeping an inventory that leaders can actually trust. It is designed for SMB environments that need real visibility without building a bureaucracy worthy of a small empire.
Quarterly or upon major tool change
IT, security, procurement, privacy, business leads

sections.heading: How to use this checklist
bullets: Use it during discovery, onboarding, periodic review, or cleanup exercises for AI tools already in circulation.
Record gaps rather than guessing. An inventory with honest blanks is still better than a fake masterpiece built on assumptions.
Any tool that is unapproved, handling sensitive data, or linked to external-facing decisions should trigger deeper review.
Completed items should feed the AI Use Case Registry, procurement review, or shadow AI workflow as appropriate.

sections.heading: 1. Set inventory scope and ownership
paragraphs: Before collecting tool names, decide who owns the exercise, what counts as an AI tool, and what business areas are in scope.
tables: Confirm the inventory owner and supporting reviewers.
Identify the lead function and any supporting roles from IT, security, procurement, privacy, legal, or operations.
Define what qualifies as an AI tool for this inventory.
Include generative AI apps, AI-enabled SaaS features, copilots, embedded assistants, APIs, model services, workflow automations, and browser extensions if relevant.
Define the business units, geographies, or environments in scope.
Clarify whether the exercise covers the whole company, a pilot group, a region, or only production environments.
Examples: baseline discovery, annual recertification, procurement support, policy enforcement, or cleanup of shadow AI.

sections.heading: 2. Identify sources of truth and discovery inputs
paragraphs: An inventory is only as good as the places you look. If discovery is lazy, the inventory will be fiction with a table border.
tables: List the main data sources for discovery.
Examples: procurement records, SaaS catalogs, expense data, browser extension reports, SSO apps, IT asset lists, business surveys, vendor lists, or contract records.
Check whether employee self-reporting is needed.
Determine where a survey, attestation, or manager confirmation is required to find tools outside formal procurement.
Review current approved tool lists or policies.
Use existing approved platform lists, banned tool lists, and exception records as comparison points.
Plan how shadow AI findings will be captured.
Decide whether they will be logged directly in the inventory, routed to a shadow AI workflow, or both.

Discovery tip
Do not rely only on procurement records. AI tools sneak in through browser plugins, trial accounts, built-in SaaS features, reimbursed expenses, and the ancient human tradition of “I thought it was fine.”

sections.heading: 3. Identify AI tools currently used or proposed
paragraphs: The goal here is visibility, not perfection. Capture the tools people are actually touching, not just the ones procurement remembers.
tables: Record each AI tool or AI-enabled feature by name.
Capture the commercial name, embedded feature name, or internal nickname used by employees.
Identify whether the tool is active, piloting, proposed, or retired.
Status helps separate experimentation from operational reliance.
Note the primary business purpose for each tool.
Examples: drafting, summarization, research, code assistance, image generation, analytics, chatbot support, workflow automation, or decision support.
Check whether the same vendor or feature appears in multiple teams.
Consolidate duplicates and distinguish enterprise deployments from scattered self-serve use.

sections.heading: 4. Record the tool profile and ownership
paragraphs: Every tool entry should have a real owner. “Everyone uses it” is not ownership. That is chaos with Wi-Fi.
tables: Identify the business owner for each tool.
Capture the accountable owner, not just the first person who answered an email.
Identify the technical or administrative owner if different.
Note the platform owner, admin team, or IT contact responsible for setup, access, and support.
Record vendor name, plan type, contract vehicle, enterprise tenant status, API usage, or local-only deployment if applicable.
Identify which departments, roles, or external parties can access or rely on the tool.

sections.heading: 5. Capture data exposure and integration risk
paragraphs: This section separates harmless curiosity from the kind of tool usage that makes security, privacy, and leadership suddenly very interested.
tables: Document the types of data entered into or exposed to the tool.
Examples: public information, internal business data, customer data, employee data, financial data, code, regulated data, or uploaded files.
Identify the highest sensitivity level involved.
Capture the most sensitive data reasonably expected to touch the tool.
Check whether the tool integrates with internal systems or repositories.
Examples: email, file shares, CRM, ERP, HRIS, ticketing systems, or knowledge bases.
Capture retention, reuse, and training assumptions.
Document whether data may be stored, logged, reused for training, or deleted on request based on available vendor information.

Watch this closely
If users cannot say what data goes into the tool, how the output is used, or whether the vendor reuses prompts or files for model training, the entry is not mature enough to trust.

sections.heading: 6. Confirm approval status and policy alignment
paragraphs: A useful inventory tells you not only what exists, but whether it is allowed, tolerated, unknown, or already drifting into trouble.
tables: Mark whether the tool is approved, conditionally approved, unapproved, or unknown.
Tie each status to the current policy or review record where possible.
Check whether an exception or waiver exists.
Record any temporary approvals, pilot exceptions, or risk acceptances tied to the tool.
Confirm whether the tool aligns with the AI acceptable use policy.
Flag any mismatch involving prohibited data, prohibited use, unsupported features, or missing guardrails.
Identify whether procurement and legal review have occurred.
Note whether a contract, DPA, security review, or vendor risk review exists.

sections.heading: 7. Assign risk indicators and escalation triggers
paragraphs: Not every tool needs a drama soundtrack. But some absolutely need a deeper look before the company gets surprised in public.
tables: Examples: customer-facing interactions, employee-impacting outputs, significant decision support, automated actions, or sensitive-data exposure.
Examples: self-procured use, unsanctioned browser extensions, consumer tools used for work, or usage discovered outside normal channels.
Determine whether deeper review is required.
Identify if the tool should go to procurement review, vendor risk review, privacy assessment, or incident follow-up.
Assign an initial risk rating or priority level.
Low, medium, and high is enough for a baseline inventory unless your organization enjoys unnecessary complexity as a hobby.

Escalation note
Any tool that touches sensitive data, influences real-world decisions, or was discovered outside normal approval channels should be routed for deeper governance review.

sections.heading: 8. Confirm operational controls and maintenance
paragraphs: Inventories age fast. A good entry should say who maintains it, how access is controlled, and when the record must be reviewed again.
tables: Confirm who grants and removes access.
Document the admin path, SSO controls, licensing owner, or local approval process.
Check whether monitoring, logging, or usage reporting exists.
Identify what visibility the organization has into adoption, misuse, or unusual behavior.
Confirm required training or user guidance exists.
State whether users have policy guidance, do-not-enter rules, or output review instructions.
Set a review trigger or recertification date.
Use dates or events such as contract renewal, feature expansion, data-type changes, wider rollout, or incident findings.

sections.heading: 9. Prepare the inventory entry for action
paragraphs: By the end of the checklist, each tool should be ready to remain in service, go through review, be monitored more closely, or be shut down with dignity.
tables: Define the next action for the tool entry.
Examples: retain as approved, route for review, remediate controls, consolidate duplicates, restrict usage, or retire.
Confirm the inventory record is complete enough for leadership or governance reporting.
The record should support real decisions, not just a tally count.
Link the tool to related governance artifacts.
Reference the AI Use Case Registry, procurement review, incident record, acceptable use exception, or shadow AI case if applicable.
Capture the final notes or unresolved gaps.
Document what still needs to be solved, by whom, and by when.

sections.heading: 10. Final inventory readiness and handoff
paragraphs: Use this closing section before the tool record is marked complete, entered into the registry, or routed for escalation.
tables: The tool record is complete enough to support governance decisions.
Tool name, owner, purpose, users, vendor details, data exposure, approval status, and next action are documented.
Duplicate entries and conflicting names have been resolved.
The same tool is not hiding under five names like it is running from taxes.
Shadow AI, sensitive-data handling, public-facing uses, or procurement gaps have been routed correctly.
The inventory entry is ready for the AI Use Case Registry or related control records.
Registry ID, review date, risk rating, and decision status are ready to be logged.
A next review date or monitoring trigger has been assigned.
Set the trigger for periodic recertification, vendor change, new data use, broader rollout, or issue-driven review.

sections.heading: AI Training & Role-Based Awareness Checklist
paragraphs: Template • Version 1.0 • 2026-04-03

sections.heading: Purpose
paragraphs: Use this checklist to validate completion of required governance activities and maintain an audit-ready record.

sections.heading: How to Use
paragraphs: Mark each item as completed (☒) when done. Add owner, due date, and evidence link/location for audit traceability.

sections.heading: Audience Mapping
tables: Audience groups defined (business users, builders, reviewers/approvers, executives)

sections.heading: Core Training Content
tables: Prompt hygiene and secure usage covered
Key AI risks covered (hallucinations, bias, injection, data leakage)
Escalation and incident reporting process covered

sections.heading: Delivery & Tracking

sections.heading: Metrics & Continuous Improvement
tables: Metrics defined (completion %, repeat incidents, policy violations)
Targeted refresh training triggered for high-risk teams or repeat issues

cleanup.

approval readiness. It is designed for SMB governance teams that want discipline without red tape.

1. Overview
This intake form is the standard entry point for reviewing AI use cases before approval, expansion, or
material change. It creates a consistent record for business, IT, security, privacy, legal, and compliance
reviewers.
2. When to use this form
 New AI tools, copilots, agents, assistants, or AI-enabled features.
 Material changes to an already approved use case, such as new data types, new vendors, new users, or
customer-facing deployment.
 Third-party AI tools that will process company, employee, customer, supplier, or partner data.
 Pilots, proofs of concept, or experiments that may move into production.

 The reviewer or governance lead completes Sections I-K during triage.

justification.

sections.heading: AI Use Case Registry
paragraphs: Standard operating template for documenting, reviewing, approving, and monitoring organizational AI use cases.
Use this template as a controlled document and update it whenever a new AI use case is proposed, materially changed, approved, retired, or escalated.
tables: Purpose  This registry is the system of record for all material AI use cases in the organization. It helps leadership see what AI is being used, by whom, for what purpose, at what risk level, and under what approval status.

sections.heading: 1. Registry Overview
paragraphs: The AI Use Case Registry is the organization’s central control record for AI usage. It supports visibility, review, accountability, and evidence of governance decisions. Every approved, pending, escalated, prohibited, or retired AI use case should be captured here.
Recommended operating rule:
No material AI use case should go live without being logged in the registry and assigned an owner, a risk rating, and an approval status.
tables: Use case name, business purpose, owner, tool/vendor, data involved, affected users, risk level, review outcome, and monitoring date.
Creates one source of truth. Prevents “we thought someone else was tracking it” syndrome.
Business owner with AI Governance Lead oversight.
New use case, material change, re-review, incident, vendor change, or retirement.

sections.heading: 2. Completion Instructions
paragraphs: • Create one row for each distinct AI use case, not one row per employee or per prompt.
• Log both proposed and active use cases so the organization can see its AI pipeline as well as current exposure.
• Where one tool supports multiple materially different use cases, record them separately.
• Update the registry when data types, business purpose, vendor, risk level, or approval status materially change.
• Use the Review Notes and Required Controls fields to document conditions of approval, limitations, or remediation actions.
Status definitions
tables: Submitted and awaiting triage or review.
Complete intake and assign initial risk rating.
Approved for use under stated conditions.
Operate, monitor, and revalidate as scheduled.
Approved only if listed controls or restrictions are followed.
Track remediation, guardrails, and owner actions.
Requires deeper review due to higher risk or uncertainty.
Route to security, privacy, legal, or leadership review.
Stop or prevent deployment and document rationale.
Keep record for history and audit trail.

sections.heading: 3. Required Registry Fields
paragraphs: Use the following field set as the baseline standard. Add columns only when they serve a specific business or regulatory need.
tables: Unique identifier for tracking and cross-reference.
Short business-facing name of the AI use case.
Named person accountable for the use case outcome.
Primary function using or sponsoring the use case.
AI platform, model, or vendor used.
What the use case does and why the business wants it.
Who uses it or is affected by it.
Main data categories used as inputs or context.
Whether personal, confidential, regulated, or restricted data is involved.
Whether outputs affect customers, employees, or third parties.
Low, Medium, High, or Critical based on triage criteria.
Current decision state of the use case.
Controls, restrictions, or guardrails attached to approval.
No personal data; human review required
Date of latest review or decision.
Concise rationale, issues, escalation notes, or remediation actions.

sections.heading: 4. Risk Rating Guidance
paragraphs: Use a simple risk-based approach. Small businesses do not need a PhD thesis to decide whether a use case is risky. They need clear triggers and defensible judgment.
tables: Internal drafting, summarization, or brainstorming with non-sensitive content.
Internal automation, internal confidential data in approved systems, or non-material decisions.
Sensitive data, customer-facing outputs, HR/finance/legal impacts, or significant operational dependence.
Escalated review with privacy, legal, and security input.
Automated decisions with major impact, regulated use, safety impact, or unresolved vendor/data concerns.
Executive review, enhanced controls, or prohibition.

sections.heading: 5. Master Registry Template
paragraphs: Use this page as the formal template. For active operations, the same structure can also be maintained in a spreadsheet or system of record.
tables: Practical tip: If the registry grows beyond a handful of entries, move the live version into a spreadsheet or governance platform. Keep this document as the standard and control reference.

sections.heading: 6. Example Entries
paragraphs: These examples show what “good enough” documentation looks like for common SMB use cases.
tables: No sensitive personal data. Human review required before external sharing.
Only approved tenant. No payment card or health data. Responses reviewed by agent.
Potential employment impact. Requires legal/privacy review before rollout.

sections.heading: 7. Approval and Control Record
paragraphs: Use this block to evidence ownership of the registry standard itself. Operational approval of individual use cases should be captured in the registry rows and supporting workflow.
Template note: Replace bracketed placeholders and adapt terminology to your internal governance model. Keep field names stable over time to support trend analysis and auditability.
tables: At least quarterly during rollout, then annually or upon material change

sections.heading: Vendor AI Due Diligence Checklist
paragraphs: A practical checklist for screening, validating, and documenting third-party AI vendors before purchase, renewal, or expanded deployment.
tables:
Purpose
Use this checklist to confirm that an AI vendor has been properly profiled, that data handling and security controls are understood, that AI-specific risks have been addressed, and that the organization can defend its buying decision if leadership, customers, auditors, or common sense ask hard questions later.
Before purchase, pilot, renewal, or expansion
Procurement, IT, security, privacy, legal, business sponsor

sections.heading: How to use this checklist
bullets: Use it as a process checklist, not as a substitute for judgment. A completed checklist with no thinking behind it is just paperwork cardio.
Capture evidence as you go: links, questionnaires, contracts, security reports, architecture notes, and remediation commitments.
Any vendor touching sensitive data, core business processes, or external-facing decisions should trigger deeper review before approval.
Open gaps do not always mean “do not buy,” but they do mean “do not pretend.” Track them and assign conditions, owners, and due dates.

sections.heading: Checklist flow and decision guidance
paragraphs: The checklist is organized in the order a sensible team would usually review a vendor: establish context, understand data and security posture, review AI-specific governance, then land the decision cleanly.
tables: Business need, sponsor, deployment path, contract timing
Data types, access model, storage, security controls, legal terms
Model governance, training practices, human oversight, drift and change control
Residual risks, remediation conditions, approval path, next actions

sections.heading: Suggested dispositions
tables: Low residual risk and controls are acceptable
Proceed with normal onboarding and register the vendor in the relevant inventory.
Document conditions, due dates, owners, and any usage restrictions before rollout.
Material risk, unclear evidence, or high-impact use case
Escalate to security, privacy, legal, executive sponsor, or AI governance forum.
Unacceptable risk or unresolved control weakness
Do not buy, renew, or expand until the issue is materially addressed.

Minimum evidence to request
At a minimum, request the vendor questionnaire or equivalent written responses, security and privacy documentation, architecture or data flow summary, contract terms, subprocessors list, incident response commitments, and any AI-specific governance material the vendor can produce without fainting.

sections.heading: 1. Engagement context and vendor profile
paragraphs: Confirm the organization knows what is being bought, who wants it, why it matters, and how the vendor fits into the environment before the due diligence gets lost in technical weeds.
tables: A business sponsor is identified and accountable
Business sponsor, team, and intended users are named.
The service or product being reviewed is clearly defined
Vendor name, product name, edition, modules, and deployment model are captured.
The intended use case is described in plain language
Business objective, user population, and whether the tool is internal or external facing are recorded.
New purchase, pilot, renewal, expansion, replacement, or emergency exception is identified.
Key dates and commercial timing are understood
Desired go-live, contract timing, renewal date, and any urgent decision points are captured.
Planned integrations, SSO, API access, data connectors, or embedded model usage are listed.
The vendor has a named point of contact for diligence
Sales, security, privacy, or technical contacts are available for follow-up.

sections.heading: 2. Data handling, privacy, and information lifecycle
paragraphs: This section checks whether the vendor touches the wrong data in the wrong way, which is usually how a “cool tool” becomes a compliance migraine.
tables: Data categories processed by the vendor are identified
Public, internal, confidential, regulated, customer, employee, source code, or other data types are listed.
Sensitive or regulated data exposure is explicitly assessed
PII, PHI, payment data, legal records, or other sensitive inputs are identified or ruled out.
The vendor’s data flow is understood
Where data enters, is processed, stored, transferred, and deleted is documented.
Training on customer data is confirmed or contractually restricted
Clarify whether prompts, files, telemetry, or outputs are used to train or improve the vendor’s models.
Retention and deletion terms are reviewed
Retention windows, deletion rights, backup handling, and offboarding deletion process are captured.
Subprocessors and hosting locations are disclosed
Material subprocessors, storage regions, and cross-border data transfer considerations are documented.
Privacy commitments align with intended use
DPA, privacy notice, lawful processing basis, and data subject rights support are reviewed where applicable.

sections.heading: 3. Security, identity, access, and resilience
paragraphs: This section covers the controls that keep the vendor from becoming an open side door into your environment or a black box that collapses the first time something goes wrong.
tables: Identity and authentication model is reviewed
SSO support, MFA, local account controls, service accounts, and provisioning methods are documented.
Role-based access and admin control model are understood
Privilege levels, admin roles, separation of duties, and access restriction capabilities are captured.
Security assurance evidence is requested and reviewed
SOC 2, ISO 27001, penetration tests, certifications, or security whitepapers are gathered.
Encryption in transit and at rest, key management approach, and tenant segregation position are reviewed.
Logging and audit visibility are available or planned
Admin logs, user activity logs, API logs, and export options are confirmed.
Incident response and breach commitments are documented
Notification terms, response obligations, and support paths for incidents are captured.
Business continuity and service resilience are assessed
Availability commitments, backups, failover, disaster recovery, and support model are reviewed.

sections.heading: 4. AI-specific governance and model risk controls
paragraphs: A normal SaaS review is not enough when the vendor’s product uses AI. This section tests whether the vendor can explain how the magic trick works without acting offended that you asked.
tables: The AI capability used in the product is clearly described
Foundation model, embedded feature, agentic workflow, retrieval system, or custom model usage is identified.
Confirm whether outputs require review before action and whether the tool supports reviewer checkpoints.
High-impact decision use is identified or prohibited
Clarify whether outputs influence hiring, legal, financial, safety, customer, or regulated decisions.
Testing, validation, or quality control practices are described
Vendor-provided evaluation methods, benchmark claims, or quality monitoring processes are captured.
How the vendor handles model upgrades, feature changes, release notes, and customer notice is reviewed.
Bias, safety, and misuse safeguards are addressed
Content filtering, guardrails, abuse prevention, safety mitigations, and known limitations are documented.
Explainability, transparency, or documentation is sufficient for the use case
Assess whether the vendor provides enough information for the organization to trust and govern the output.

sections.heading: 5. Legal, contractual, and commercial review
paragraphs: This section confirms the contract does not quietly undo the control posture you thought you had five pages ago.
tables: Contract terms align with the intended risk posture
Master terms, order form, and any AI-specific addenda are reviewed.
Data ownership and usage rights are clear
The organization retains ownership of data, outputs, and restricted use rights are documented.
Contract language protects prompts, uploaded files, outputs, and related business information.
Audit, assurance, or evidence rights are understood
Determine whether the organization can obtain updated reports, questionnaires, or notice of material changes.
Liability, indemnity, and limitations are reviewed
Commercial and legal stakeholders assess whether the balance is acceptable for the use case.
Termination and exit terms are workable
Data export, deletion support, notice periods, and dependency risks are reviewed.
Pricing and commercial model do not introduce hidden risk
Metering, usage caps, overage behavior, and expansion assumptions are understood.

sections.heading: 6. Implementation readiness, conditions, and handoff
paragraphs: Before anything is approved, make sure the organization is actually ready to deploy the vendor without inventing controls later under stress.
tables: Identify who will lead onboarding, configuration, and control implementation.
Required technical controls are known before purchase
SSO, tenant configuration, logging, role restrictions, DLP, or network restrictions are identified.
User guidance or acceptable use needs are identified
Determine whether employees need training, policy updates, or restricted use guidance.
Summarize open issues, caveats, and assumptions rather than burying them in side comments.
Conditions of use are defined if approval is conditional
List restricted data types, prohibited use cases, compensating controls, or time-boxed approvals.
Registry and inventory updates are planned
Confirm where the vendor, tool, and related use cases will be recorded after approval.
Renewal or re-review triggers are defined
Set what would trigger re-review: new integrations, new data types, major model change, incident, or expanded scope.

sections.heading: 7. Final review, conditions, and sign-off
paragraphs: Use this page to close the loop, document residual risk, and make the decision readable for the next person who inherits the file instead of guessing what happened.
tables: Approve / Approve w/ conditions / Escalate / Decline

sections.heading: Approval and conditions checklist
tables: Decision authority is clearly identified before procurement proceeds
Name the approver or forum that owns the final decision.
Any conditions of approval are documented in writing
List conditions, restrictions, and due dates rather than trusting future memory.
Contract, privacy, and security commitments are stored with the review record
Reference the contract file, security packet, or repository location.
Vendor onboarding or rollout steps are aligned to the final decision

tables: The AI system does not perform as intended or expected.
For example, model has inaccurate predictions, model drift (decay), underperformance in real-world scenarios.
Poor data quality, flawed design, inadequate testing or updates.
The AI system causes harm to people, environment or property.
For example, autonomous vehicle accidents, unsafe medical device diagnosis.

A robust model behaves consistently across a wide range of inputs, including those that are noisy, incomplete, or slightly different from its training data. An insufficiently robust model might behave erratically or incorrectly if even small variations occur.
The AI system is vulnerable to attacks or manipulation.
For example, the model is susceptible to adversarial attacks, data poisoning, model theft.
Inadequate access control, lack of threat modeling, unmonitored deployment.
Lack of clarity around how the AI system makes its decisions and who is responsible for its actions.

For example, an AI system makes a discriminatory lending decision, but no team or individual can explain or take responsibility for why it happened. Or an AI system recommends denying treatment without a clear explanation. Doctors can’t challenge or verify the decision, eroding trust and safety.

Organizational design flaw — no one is formally responsible for the AI’s behavior or outcomes. Also Technical design flaw — information about the AI system and its outputs is not
available to individuals interacting with the system.
Humans cannot understand how or why the AI system made a decision.
For example, Black-box models used in healthcare or criminal justice. No one knows how the conclusion was reached.
Complex or opaque model architectures without supporting tools.
AI system exposes or infers personal, confidential, or sensitive data.
For example, re-identification of anonymized data or data leakage.
Poor data handling, weak privacy-preserving mechanisms, model inversion (i.e. attacker reconstructs sensitive information about the training data).
AI outcomes result in unfair treatment or discrimination.
For example, credit scoring models denying loans to minorities.
Biased training data, unbalanced features, lack of fairness audits.

sections.heading: Summary
tables: Organization lacks a defined governance structure, policies, or controls to effectively manage the risks associated with the development, deployment, and use of AI systems.

The absence of foundational risk management capabilities may result in inconsistent oversight, regulatory non-compliance, ethical breaches, operational failures, and reputational damage as AI technologies are increasingly integrated into business processes.

For example, a mid-sized bank deploys an AI-powered customer service chatbot trained on historical call center transcripts to improve response times. However, the organization lacks a formal AI governance framework—there are no AI-specific policies, no defined ownership for AI oversight, and no pre-deployment risk assessments in place. After deployment, the chatbot begins providing inaccurate financial advice (e.g., misquoting loan rates or account terms), mishandling sensitive personal data, and demonstrating inconsistent responses to customers of different backgrounds, raising concerns about bias.

Lack of organizational awareness or executive ownership of AI risk, rapid adoption of AI technologies without enterprise-wide risk alignment. Absence of formal AI governance policies, roles, or accountability mechanisms.

Establish and maintain a comprehensive AI governance framework that defines organizational roles, policies, procedures, and control mechanisms to effectively oversee the development, deployment, and use of AI systems
AI system may fail to perform as intended or expected, producing inconsistent, inaccurate, or unstable outcomes across different conditions or over time.

This risk refers to the AI model's inability to maintain accuracy or consistency in its predictions or outputs. It may perform well during testing but fail in real-world scenarios due to factors such as data drift (perfomance worsens over time because real world data has changed over time), insufficient training data, or lack of generalization. This undermines user trust and could lead to poor decision-making, especially in critical areas like finance, healthcare, or safety systems.
For example, model has inaccurate predictions, model drift (decay), underperformance in real-world scenarios.
Poor data quality, flawed design, inadequate testing or updates.
Ensure that AI systems are designed, tested, and monitored to consistently perform as intended, producing accurate and reliable outcomes throughout their lifecycle.
AI system may cause physical, psychological, or environmental harm due to unsafe outputs or actions.
AI safety risks arise when a system behaves unpredictably or in ways that create harm—especially in autonomous or high-stakes applications (e.g., self-driving vehicles, medical devices).
For example, autonomous vehicle accidents, unsafe medical device diagnosis.

A robust model should behave consistently across a wide range of inputs, including those that are noisy, incomplete, or slightly different from its training data. An insufficiently robust model might behave erratically or incorrectly if even small variations occur.
Ensure that AI systems are developed and deployed with safeguards that minimize the risk of physical, psychological, or environmental harm under both normal and adverse conditions.
AI system may be vulnerable to malicious attacks, manipulation, or systemic failures that compromise its integrity, availability, or confidentiality.

AI systems can be targets of adversarial attacks (e.g., manipulated inputs), data poisoning, or unauthorized access. If not properly secured, they may expose sensitive data, produce misleading outputs, or become inoperable during critical operations. Resilience also involves the system's ability to recover from faults or disruptions. Failure in this area exposes organizations to cyber risk, service outages, and compliance breaches.
For example, the model is susceptible to adversarial attacks, data poisoning, model theft.
Inadequate access control, lack of threat modeling, unmonitored deployment.
Ensure that AI systems are protected against threats, adversarial attacks, and system failures, and are capable of maintaining functionality or recovering rapidly from disruptions.
AI system may lack clear accountability for outcomes or operate in a manner that is opaque to stakeholders and regulators.

When it's unclear who is responsible for AI behavior—or if the system’s decision-making process cannot be traced—accountability breaks down. This is especially problematic when decisions impact individuals’ rights or are subject to legal scrutiny. A lack of transparency can lead to non-compliance with emerging regulations (like the EU AI Act) and erode trust with customers, regulators, and the public.

For example, an AI system makes a discriminatory lending decision, but no team or individual can explain or take responsibility for why it happened. Or an AI system recommends denying treatment without a clear explanation. Doctors can’t challenge or verify the decision, eroding trust and safety.

Organizational design flaw — no one is formally responsible for the AI’s behavior or outcomes. Also Technical design flaw — information about the AI system and its outputs is not
available to individuals interacting with the system.
Ensure that clear accountability for AI system outcomes exist through defined roles, responsibilities, and documentation, and the logic around decision-making logic is transparent.
AI system may generate decisions or outputs that are difficult for humans to understand, evaluate, or challenge.

If users cannot understand how or why the AI arrived at a certain outcome (especially in high-risk sectors), it undermines trust and the ability to correct or audit the system. Explainability is crucial for human oversight, regulatory reviews, and ethical assurance. “Black box” models pose significant governance risks when their behavior cannot be interpreted by decision-makers.
For example, Black-box models used in healthcare or criminal justice. No one knows how the conclusion was reached.
Complex or opaque model architectures without supporting tools.
Ensure that AI system outputs are explainable and interpretable by stakeholders, enabling meaningful understanding, effective oversight, and trust in decision-making processes.
The AI system may expose, infer, or misuse personal or sensitive data, violating privacy laws or expectations.

AI models trained on personal data may inadvertently leak private information or infer sensitive attributes (e.g., health status, political views). This includes risks of model inversion, re-identification, or over-collection of data. These privacy failures can lead to legal penalties (e.g., under GDPR or CCPA), loss of consumer trust, and ethical concerns.
For example, re-identification of anonymized data or data leakage.
Poor data handling, weak privacy-preserving mechanisms, model inversion (i.e. attacker reconstructs sensitive information about the training data).
Ensure that AI systems are designed and operated with privacy-preserving techniques that protect individuals’ personal and sensitive data from unauthorized use, inference, or disclosure.
The AI system may produce unfair, biased, or discriminatory outcomes that disadvantage certain groups.

Bias in AI can arise from imbalanced or unrepresentative training data, flawed design, or societal patterns embedded in data. This leads to performance disparities that disproportionately harm protected groups—e.g., gender or racial bias in hiring, lending, or criminal justice tools. Managing harmful bias is critical to ensuring fairness, legal compliance (e.g., civil rights laws), and ethical alignment.
For example, credit scoring models denying loans to minorities.
Biased training data, unbalanced features, lack of fairness audits.
Ensure that AI systems are proactively assessed and mitigated for harmful bias, promoting fairness and equity in outcomes across diverse populations and contexts.
Content generated by AI system may lack clear ownership or infringe on existing intellectual property rights due to the use of copyrighted materials.

Legal and compliance issues that arise when AI-generated content (e.g., images, text, music, code) lacks a clearly defined owner under current copyright laws or the AI system was trained on copyrighted data without proper licensing, causing the outputs to replicate or derive from protected works, potentially infringing on intellectual property (IP) rights.

For example, an AI company developed a text-to-image model that allows users to generate digital artwork based on text prompts. Some artists discovered that the AI-generated images replicated their distinctive style and even contained identifiable elements from their original works. These artists had not consented to their works being used for training, nor were they compensated or credited. Lawsuits were filed against the AI company

AI systems are often trained on large-scale datasets that include copyrighted content—such as images, text, code, music, and videos—without explicit rights, permissions, or documentation. This leads to models that can unintentionally reproduce or mimic protected works, and generate content with unclear legal authorship or IP attribution.
Ensure that AI systems are trained on datasets that are properly sourced, licensed, and documented to confirm compliance with copyright and intellectual property laws.

This control is about limiting the scope and speed of AI actions to reduce the chance of harm. It acts like a digital seatbelt, ensuring that even if the AI behaves unexpectedly, the impact is contained and manageable.

1. Identify high-risk behaviors the AI system could exhibit (e.g., rapid decision-making, harmful outputs, system overreach).
Determine where constraints are needed—on speed, content, or environment.
2. Define Safety Constraints:
-Rate Limiting: Set thresholds for how often the AI can act (e.g., decisions per second, API calls per user).
-Response Bounding: Define acceptable output formats, ranges, and content (e.g., no medical advice, profanity filters).
-Sandboxing: Determine which components or models should be isolated for testing or limited deployment.
3: Design and Implement Controls
-Rate Limiting: Use throttling mechanisms (e.g., token buckets, leaky buckets), Apply per-user, per-IP, or per-function limits.
-Response Bounding: Add validation layers to check outputs before delivery, Use rule-based filters or classifiers to block unsafe content.
-Sandboxing: Deploy new models in isolated environments, Use synthetic or anonymized data for testing, Restrict access to external systems or users.
Ensure that AI systems are developed and deployed with safeguards that minimize the risk of physical, psychological, or environmental harm under both normal and adverse conditions.

This control is about being proactive, not reactive. It ensures that when AI systems fail or behave unsafely, your organization is ready to respond quickly, contain the issue, and recover safely—minimizing harm to users, systems, and society.

1. Identify potential safety-related incidents (e.g., harmful outputs, model drift, adversarial attacks, data leakage).
2. Create a structured response plan tailored to AI systems. Include:
-Detection: How incidents are identified (e.g., monitoring, alerts)
-Containment: Immediate actions to limit harm (e.g., shutdown, rollback)
-Communication: Who is notified and how
-Resolution: Steps to fix the issue
-Post-incident review: Root cause analysis and lessons learned
3. Assign Roles and Responsibilities
4. Integrate AI response plan with org-wide incident response plan
5. Test the Response Plan

Apply rate limiting, response bounding, or sandboxing to minimize harm from live AI systems.

This control is about limiting the scope and speed of AI actions to reduce the chance of harm. It acts like a digital seatbelt, ensuring that even if the AI behaves unexpectedly, the impact is contained and manageable.

1. Identify high-risk behaviors the AI system could exhibit (e.g., rapid decision-making, harmful outputs, system overreach).
Determine where constraints are needed—on speed, content, or environment.
2. Define Safety Constraints:
-Rate Limiting: Set thresholds for how often the AI can act (e.g., decisions per second, API calls per user).
-Response Bounding: Define acceptable output formats, ranges, and content (e.g., no medical advice, profanity filters).
-Sandboxing: Determine which components or models should be isolated for testing or limited deployment.
3: Design and Implement Controls
-Rate Limiting: Use throttling mechanisms (e.g., token buckets, leaky buckets), Apply per-user, per-IP, or per-function limits.
-Response Bounding: Add validation layers to check outputs before delivery, Use rule-based filters or classifiers to block unsafe content.
-Sandboxing: Deploy new models in isolated environments, Use synthetic or anonymized data for testing, Restrict access to external systems or users.
Ensure that AI systems are developed and deployed with safeguards that minimize the risk of physical, psychological, or environmental harm under both normal and adverse conditions.
Develop and test AI-specific incident response procedures for safety-related events.

This control is about being proactive, not reactive. It ensures that when AI systems fail or behave unsafely, your organization is ready to respond quickly, contain the issue, and recover safely—minimizing harm to users, systems, and society.

1. Identify potential safety-related incidents (e.g., harmful outputs, model drift, adversarial attacks, data leakage).
2. Create a structured response plan tailored to AI systems. Include:
-Detection: How incidents are identified (e.g., monitoring, alerts)
-Containment: Immediate actions to limit harm (e.g., shutdown, rollback)
-Communication: Who is notified and how
-Resolution: Steps to fix the issue
-Post-incident review: Root cause analysis and lessons learned
3. Assign Roles and Responsibilities
4. Integrate AI response plan with org-wide incident response plan
5. Test the Response Plan

tables: Ensure that AI systems are protected against threats, adversarial attacks, and system failures, and are capable of maintaining functionality or recovering rapidly from disruptions.

Identify AI assets (e.g., models, training data, APIs).
Map potential attack surfaces (e.g., model inversion, data poisoning).
Use frameworks like STRIDE or MITRE ATLAS tailored for AI.
Document threats, vulnerabilities, and mitigations.
Review and update regularly.

Break down the AI lifecycle: data collection, training, deployment, monitoring.
Identify risks at each phase (e.g., insecure data pipelines, exposed APIs).
Score risks based on likelihood and impact.
Prioritize and assign mitigation actions.
Reassess after major changes or incidents.

Harden OS, containers, and cloud environments hosting AI.

GS-PLBK-90DAYBLU-01.title: 90-Day AI Governance Implementation Blueprint

sections.heading: Implementation Blueprint
paragraphs: A Step-by-Step Rollout Plan for SMBs
Version 1.0  |  April 2026  |  Confidential – Internal Use Only
tables: Govsecure AI Governance Platform  |  Practical AI Governance for SMBs

sections.heading: Executive Summary
paragraphs: This 90-Day AI Governance Implementation Blueprint provides Govsecure clients with a structured, actionable roadmap for standing up a complete AI governance program from scratch. Designed specifically for small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs), it transforms the comprehensive Govsecure AI Governance Checklist Templates into a phased sequence of concrete activities, ownership assignments, and measurable milestones.

At the end of 90 days, your organization will have a live AI inventory, a documented policy framework, completed risk assessments for active AI systems, trained staff, and a repeatable operational rhythm for ongoing AI governance—all aligned to NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and emerging regulatory requirements.

sections.heading: What This Blueprint Delivers
tables: Every AI tool, model, and vendor feature documented and risk-tiered.
Foundational policies covering use, risk, privacy, and vendor management.
Completed risk assessments for all Active/High-risk AI use cases.
Documented assessments for all AI-enabled vendors and SaaS tools.
Reviewer roles, escalation paths, and override procedures in place.
AI-specific IR procedures tested and embedded in existing processes.
Role-based AI literacy and governance training completed across key staff.
Recurring review schedule established with assigned owners.

Govsecure Tip:  AI governance does not require a large team or a big budget. It requires clarity, consistency, and accountability. This blueprint is designed so that a single part-time program owner, with cross-functional support, can complete it on schedule.

sections.heading: How to Use This Blueprint
paragraphs: This blueprint is organized into three 30-day phases, each broken down by week. Every week includes a structured activity table referencing the specific Govsecure checklist template that drives the work. Use it alongside your Govsecure checklist files—not as a replacement for them.

sections.heading: Core Roles
tables: Owns the program, drives the blueprint, reports to leadership.
Submit use cases, confirm AI tools in their area, review risk tiers.
Provide technical context, conduct security reviews, manage integrations.

sections.heading: Implementation Blueprint
paragraphs: A Step-by-Step Rollout Plan for SMBs
Version 1.0  |  April 2026  |  Confidential – Internal Use Only
tables: Confident AI, Controlled Risk.   ·   govsecure.ai   ·   AI Governance Platform for SMBs

sections.heading: Executive Summary
paragraphs: This 90-Day AI Governance Implementation Blueprint provides Govsecure clients with a structured, actionable roadmap for standing up a complete AI governance program from scratch. Designed specifically for small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs), it transforms the comprehensive Govsecure AI Governance Checklist Templates into a phased sequence of concrete activities, ownership assignments, and measurable milestones.

At the end of 90 days, your organization will have a live AI inventory, a documented policy framework, completed risk assessments for active AI systems, trained staff, and a repeatable operational rhythm for ongoing AI governance—all aligned to NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and emerging regulatory requirements.

sections.heading: What This Blueprint Delivers
tables: Every AI tool, model, and vendor feature documented and risk-tiered.
Foundational policies covering use, risk, privacy, and vendor management.
Completed risk assessments for all Active/High-risk AI use cases.
Documented assessments for all AI-enabled vendors and SaaS tools.
Reviewer roles, escalation paths, and override procedures in place.
AI-specific IR procedures tested and embedded in existing processes.
Role-based AI literacy and governance training completed across key staff.
Recurring review schedule established with assigned owners.

Govsecure Tip:  AI governance does not require a large team or a big budget. It requires clarity, consistency, and accountability. This blueprint is designed so that a single part-time program owner, with cross-functional support, can complete it on schedule.

sections.heading: How to Use This Blueprint
paragraphs: This blueprint is organized into three 30-day phases, each broken down by week. Every week includes a structured activity table referencing the specific Govsecure checklist template that drives the work. Use it alongside your Govsecure checklist files—not as a replacement for them.

sections.heading: Page 2
paragraphs:
1. Executive Overview
What AI Chef is
GovSecure AI Chef™ is a branded operating model for SMB AI governance. It converts AI risk, control,
ownership, and assurance into a recipe system that busy operators can actually use.
Why SMBs need this
 SMBs are adopting AI faster than they are defining ownership, review paths, and acceptable uses.
 Most governance materials are either too abstract for operations or too enterprise-heavy for lean
teams.
 Paid buyers want something they can drop into a live program without needing a six-week translation
exercise.
Primary buyer profile
SMB owners, operators, and lean technology or compliance leaders who need AI guardrails without
building a giant bureaucracy.
What the paid edition adds
 Realistic sample data so teams understand what good looks like.
 Template pages with clear required fields, review cycles, and evidence expectations.
 Appendices that make the toolkit feel implementation-ready rather than decorative.
2. Five-step adoption flow

sections.heading: Page 7
paragraphs:
5. AI Use Case Intake and Risk Tiering
Realistic sample intake
Use case: Customer support co-pilot that drafts responses, summarizes tickets, and suggests refund
options to human agents.
Field Example entry
Business purpose Reduce average ticket handling time by 20% while
keeping a human agent in the loop.
Users Support agents and team leads.
Data used Customer emails, order history, product knowledge
base, refund policy snippets.
External impact Customer-facing outputs may be sent after agent
approval.
Decision type Recommendations only; no autonomous refunds.
Regulatory relevance Privacy review required because customer data
and retention are involved.
Human oversight Mandatory agent approval before send or action.
Proposed risk tier Tier 2 - Moderate, with Tier 3 controls if refund
automation is later enabled.
Risk tier logic
 Tier 1 - Low: internal productivity, low sensitivity, no external decision effect.
 Tier 2 - Moderate: business process support, limited customer or employee impact, strong human
review.
 Tier 3 - High: meaningful operational, legal, HR, financial, or customer impact; enhanced review
required.
 Tier 4 - Prohibited / Executive review: disallowed use or activity requiring executive decision and
documented exception.
GovSecure AI Chef™ | Paid SMB Edition | v1.1 client-ready draft

8. Monitoring, Logging, and AI Incident Response
 Monitor drift, misuse, access changes, exceptions, and failed or unusual automated actions.
 Review logs on a defined cadence rather than waiting for the fancy disaster to announce itself.
 Treat harmful outputs, privacy leaks, and connector misuse as operational incidents with owners and
evidence.
Recommended incident categories
 Harmful output
 Privacy breach
 Security exposure
 Model or prompt drift
 Unauthorized automation
 Third-party service failure

AI system: Any tool, model, workflow, or agent used to generate outputs, recommendations, or actions.
Agent: An AI-enabled workflow that can take or suggest actions using tools, connectors, or instructions.
High-impact use case: A use case with meaningful financial, legal, HR, customer, or safety impact.
Human oversight: A defined review or approval step performed by an accountable human.
Model drift: A change in model behavior or performance over time that affects output quality or
safety.
Shadow AI: AI usage outside approved tools, workflows, or review paths.
Knowledge base: The source material or repository used to ground AI responses.
Prohibited use: A disallowed use case or activity that requires an exception to proceed.

sections.heading: Page 2
paragraphs:
1. Executive Overview
What AI Chef is
GovSecure AI Chef™ is a branded operating model for SMB AI governance. It converts AI risk, control,
ownership, and assurance into a recipe system that busy operators can actually use.
Why SMBs need this
 SMBs are adopting AI faster than they are defining ownership, review paths, and acceptable uses.
 Most governance materials are either too abstract for operations or too enterprise-heavy for lean
teams.
 Paid buyers want something they can drop into a live program without needing a six-week translation
exercise.
Primary buyer profile
SMB owners, operators, and lean technology or compliance leaders who need AI guardrails without
building a giant bureaucracy.
What the paid edition adds
 Realistic sample data so teams understand what good looks like.
 Template pages with clear required fields, review cycles, and evidence expectations.
 Appendices that make the toolkit feel implementation-ready rather than decorative.

sections.heading: Page 7
paragraphs:
5. AI Use Case Intake and Risk Tiering
Realistic sample intake
Use case: Customer support co-pilot that drafts responses, summarizes tickets, and suggests refund
options to human agents.
Field Example entry
Business purpose Reduce average ticket handling time by 20% while
keeping a human agent in the loop.
Users Support agents and team leads.
Data used Customer emails, order history, product knowledge
base, refund policy snippets.
External impact Customer-facing outputs may be sent after agent
approval.
Decision type Recommendations only; no autonomous refunds.
Regulatory relevance Privacy review required because customer data
and retention are involved.
Human oversight Mandatory agent approval before send or action.
Proposed risk tier Tier 2 - Moderate, with Tier 3 controls if refund
automation is later enabled.
Risk tier logic
 Tier 1 - Low: internal productivity, low sensitivity, no external decision effect.
 Tier 2 - Moderate: business process support, limited customer or employee impact, strong human
review.
 Tier 3 - High: meaningful operational, legal, HR, financial, or customer impact; enhanced review
required.
 Tier 4 - Prohibited / Executive review: disallowed use or activity requiring executive decision and
documented exception.
GovSecure AI Chef™ | Paid SMB Edition | v1.1 client-ready draft

 Monitor drift, misuse, access changes, exceptions, and failed or unusual automated actions.
 Review logs on a defined cadence rather than waiting for the fancy disaster to announce itself.
 Treat harmful outputs, privacy leaks, and connector misuse as operational incidents with owners and
evidence.

 Privacy breach
 Security exposure
 Model or prompt drift
 Unauthorized automation

Enterprise AI Governance Recipe Matrix Toolkit
v1.0 - 24 Core Recipes
A practical operational toolkit for governing AI risks, controls, ownership, and assurance across the enterprise.

sections.heading: Page 2
paragraphs: Built from the approved GovSecure AI Chef toolkit outline and template structure.

sections.heading: Page 3
paragraphs:
Executive Overview
GovSecure AI Chef turns AI governance from scattered policies into an operating model with named ownership,
risk-tiered intake, mapped controls, evidence expectations, and leadership reporting.
The toolkit is designed for SMB owners, CIO/CISO leaders, compliance teams, and AI governance practitioners
who need one framework that can support awareness, implementation, and audit readiness without becoming
theoretical sludge.
What is included
 A 24-recipe governance matrix across six stations
 An ownership and RACI model
 AI use-case intake and risk tiering workflow
 AI system registry structure
 Policy-to-control mapping baseline
 Operational guidance for data, model, vendor, agent, monitoring, and incident controls
 A maturity roadmap from 24 to 36 recipes
How to Use the Framework
Use the toolkit in five steps:
1. 1. Identify AI systems and use cases
2. 2. Assess the use case and assign a risk tier
3. 3. Assign owners and supporting functions
4. 4. Implement required controls and record evidence
5. 5. Review quarterly and improve by maturity stage

sections.heading: Page 7
paragraphs:
Ownership Model and RACI
Primary roles
 Executive Sponsor (EX): accountably backs the program and clears roadblocks.
 AI Governance Lead (AG): operates the framework, intake, reviews, and reporting.
 IT / Platform Owner (IT): manages enablement, access, integration, and platform controls.
 Security (SE): enforces security controls, monitoring, and incident coordination.
 Privacy (PR): covers personal-data handling, retention, and privacy reviews.
 Legal / Compliance (LE): addresses regulatory, contractual, and policy interpretations.
 Data Owner / Governance (DA): owns data quality, provenance, and approved access.
 Business Owner (BU): owns the use case, benefits, and outcome accountability.
 Procurement / Vendor Risk (PC): performs third-party diligence and contract coordination.
 Engineering / Product (EN): implements technical safeguards and rollout design.
Sample RACI snapshot

Head Chef A R C C I
Assignment
Approved Menu I C R A C
Only
High-Risk Use C A I C R
Case Flag
Burn Response C C R A I
Plan
Escalation path: Business Owner -> AI Governance Lead -> Security/Privacy/Legal as needed -> Executive
Sponsor for Tier 4 or exception approvals.

sections.heading: Page 10
paragraphs:
AI Incident Response Playbook
Incident categories
 Harmful or misleading output
 Privacy breach or unauthorized data exposure
 Security exposure or embedded secret disclosure
 Model or agent drift causing unsafe behavior
 Unauthorized automation or unapproved integration
 Third-party vendor or service failure
Workflow
6. Detect and triage
7. Assess severity and impacted systems
8. Contain or disable affected AI activity
9. Escalate to Security, Privacy, Legal, and Executive Sponsor as required
10. Remediate and restore safe operation
11. Review root cause, evidence, and control improvements
Minimum evidence: incident summary, owner, timeline, impacted data/systems, containment action, recovery
decision, lessons learned.

sections.heading: Page 11
paragraphs:
Governance Glossary
AI system: Any model, tool, agent, workflow, or embedded capability used to generate, classify, retrieve,
recommend, or automate decisions.
Agent: An AI-enabled system that can invoke tools, call external systems, or take actions toward a task.
High-impact use case: A use case whose output can materially affect people, finances, legal posture, privacy, or
safety.
Human oversight: A defined review or approval action by a responsible human before or after AI output is used.
Model drift: A measurable change in behavior, performance, or output quality over time that can affect
reliability or safety.
Shadow AI: Use of AI tools or capabilities outside approved governance, visibility, or control.
Knowledge base: A collection of internal or external documents and data sources used to ground or enrich AI
outputs.
Prohibited use: A use case that the organization forbids because the risk cannot be reduced to an acceptable
level.
Standards Alignment Crosswalk
Reference only - not a legal certification claim.

sections.heading: Sample SMB scenario: HarborCraft Home Goods
paragraphs: A 280-person ecommerce and operations business using Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, Zendesk AI, and two workflow automations connected to customer support and internal knowledge.

sections.heading: Accountability model
tables: Approves policy direction, high-risk exceptions, and quarterly review decisions.
Runs intake, control mapping, registry reviews, and evidence collection.
Owns use-case outcomes, human oversight, and process performance.
Handles provisioning, connector controls, logging, and technical operations.
Reviews data use, notices, and high-impact legal/privacy triggers.
Tracks vendor diligence, contract terms, and dependency issues.

sections.heading: RACI sample across five core tasks
5. AI Use Case Intake and Risk Tiering
Realistic sample intake
paragraphs: Use case: Customer support co-pilot that drafts responses, summarizes tickets, and suggests refund options to human agents.
tables: Reduce average ticket handling time by 20% while keeping a human agent in the loop.
Customer emails, order history, product knowledge base, refund policy snippets.
Customer-facing outputs may be sent after agent approval.
Privacy review required because customer data and retention are involved.
Mandatory agent approval before send or action.
Tier 2 - Moderate, with Tier 3 controls if refund automation is later enabled.

sections.heading: Risk tier logic
bullets: Tier 1 - Low: internal productivity, low sensitivity, no external decision effect.
Tier 2 - Moderate: business process support, limited customer or employee impact, strong human review.
Tier 3 - High: meaningful operational, legal, HR, financial, or customer impact; enhanced review required.
Tier 4 - Prohibited / Executive review: disallowed use or activity requiring executive decision and documented exception.

sections.heading: 6. AI System Registry - realistic examples
tables: Suggest refund risk labels before human approval

sections.heading: Registry rule of thumb
paragraphs: If a team can name the AI tool but cannot name the owner, review cycle, or permitted use, the registry entry is not complete. That gap is usually where risk starts breeding.

sections.heading: 7. Policy-to-Control Mapping
tables: Teams may use approved AI tools for low and moderate-risk tasks only.
No autonomous legal, HR, or financial commitments without explicit approval.
Human review is required before customer-facing send or high-impact action.
No new AI vendor enters production without diligence.
Sensitive data must follow classification and approved retention rules.
Prompt, action, and decision evidence must be logged where feasible.
AI incidents follow the same containment and review discipline as security incidents.
Material changes to models, connectors, or permissions must be reviewed.

sections.heading: 8. Monitoring, Logging, and AI Incident Response
bullets: Monitor drift, misuse, access changes, exceptions, and failed or unusual automated actions.
Review logs on a defined cadence rather than waiting for the fancy disaster to announce itself.
Treat harmful outputs, privacy leaks, and connector misuse as operational incidents with owners and evidence.

sections.heading: Recommended incident categories
bullets: Harmful output
Privacy breach
Security exposure
Model or prompt drift
Unauthorized automation
Third-party service failure

sections.heading: 9. Executive Dashboard and Maturity Roadmap
bullets: The next jump should focus on logging coverage, formal review notes, and tighter change control.
tables: Policy exists but ownership and intake are inconsistent.
Owners, intake, registry, and baseline controls are in place.
Review cadence, monitoring, and evidence are routine.
Controls are tested, exceptions are documented, and leadership receives clear reports.
Program is measured, improved, and scaled with confidence across business use cases.

sections.heading: Appendix A. Governance Glossary
paragraphs: AI system: Any tool, model, workflow, or agent used to generate outputs, recommendations, or actions.
Agent: An AI-enabled workflow that can take or suggest actions using tools, connectors, or instructions.
High-impact use case: A use case with meaningful financial, legal, HR, customer, or safety impact.
Human oversight: A defined review or approval step performed by an accountable human.
Model drift: A change in model behavior or performance over time that affects output quality or safety.
Shadow AI: AI usage outside approved tools, workflows, or review paths.
Knowledge base: The source material or repository used to ground AI responses.
Prohibited use: A disallowed use case or activity that requires an exception to proceed.

sections.heading: Enterprise AI Governance Recipe Matrix Toolkit
Paid SMB Edition | Client-ready operational toolkit
tables:
Who it is for

sections.heading: What AI Chef is
paragraphs: GovSecure AI Chef™ is a branded operating model for SMB AI governance. It converts AI risk, control, ownership, and assurance into a recipe system that busy operators can actually use.

sections.heading: Why SMBs need this

Most governance materials are either too abstract for operations or too enterprise-heavy for lean teams.
