GS-PLBK-AICHEFTO-13.title: GovSecure AI Chef™ — Enterprise AI Governance Recipe Matrix Toolkit — v1.0 - 24 Core Recipes — Built from the approved GovSecure AI Chef toolkit outline and template structure.

sections.heading: (preamble)
paragraphs: A practical operational toolkit for governing AI risks, controls, ownership, and assurance across the enterprise.

sections.heading: Executive Overview
paragraphs: GovSecure AI Chef turns AI governance from scattered policies into an operating model with named ownership, risk-tiered intake, mapped controls, evidence expectations, and leadership reporting.

The toolkit is designed for SMB owners, CIO/CISO leaders, compliance teams, and AI governance practitioners who need one framework that can support awareness, implementation, and audit readiness without becoming theoretical sludge.

sections.heading: What is included
bullets: A 24-recipe governance matrix across six stations
An ownership and RACI model
AI use-case intake and risk tiering workflow
AI system registry structure
Policy-to-control mapping baseline
Operational guidance for data, model, vendor, agent, monitoring, and incident controls
A maturity roadmap from 24 to 36 recipes

sections.heading: How to Use the Framework
paragraphs: Use the toolkit in five steps:
bullets: 1. Identify AI systems and use cases
2. Assess the use case and assign a risk tier
3. Assign owners and supporting functions
4. Implement required controls and record evidence
5. Review quarterly and improve by maturity stage

sections.heading: 24 Core Recipes Matrix
tables:
Head Chef Assignment
Risk: Ownership Vacuum
Assign named AI governance owner, charter, RACI, escalation authority
Set AI policy, acceptable use, prohibited use, and exception handling

Role Recipe Card
Risk: Role Confusion
Define responsibilities across IT, Security, Legal, Privacy, Data, Procurement, HR, and business

Approved Menu Only
Risk: Shadow AI
Maintain approved tools register and sanctioned use pathways

Clean Ingredients
Risk: Sensitive Data Exposure
Apply data classification and prompt handling restrictions

Source Provenance Check
Risk: Untrusted or Poor-Quality Data
Track provenance, metadata, and approved knowledge sources
Define AI-specific retention and deletion rules

Knowledge Base Boundaries
Risk: Unauthorized Retrieval
Enforce permission-aware retrieval and connector controls
Test performance, robustness, misuse, and bias before production

Secret Sauce Lockdown
Risk: Embedded Secrets Exposure
Use vaulting, secret scanning, and protected authentication
Revalidate against benchmarks and review thresholds

Vendor Pantry Review
Risk: Third-Party AI Exposure
Perform AI-specific vendor, legal, and security due diligence

Tool Permission Gate
Risk: Agent Overreach
Apply least privilege, scoped tool access, and action thresholds

Human Before High Impact
Risk: Unchecked Automation
Require human approval for high-impact actions

Sandbox Before Service
Risk: Unsafe Rollout
Use staging, pilots, rollback design, and controlled releases

Kill Switch on the Counter
Risk: Runaway Behavior
Enable emergency disablement and connector revocation

Explain the Dish
Risk: Opaque Outputs
Require rationale, notices, and traceability where appropriate

Escalate Ambiguous Orders
Risk: Harmful Outcomes
Define exception triggers and human escalation rules

Bias & Fairness Taste Test
Risk: Unfair Outcomes
Conduct fairness review and remediation for high-impact uses

High-Risk Use Case Flag
Risk: Sensitive Use Cases
Apply intake triage, risk tiering, and enhanced approval

Full Kitchen Inventory
Risk: Unknown AI Systems
Maintain AI system registry with owners, vendors, data, and status

Kitchen Camera Logs
Risk: Weak Auditability
Log prompts, tool calls, approvals, changes, exceptions, and incidents

Burn Response Plan
Risk: No AI Incident Handling

Quarterly Recipe Review
Risk: Control Decay
Review metrics, open risks, exceptions, and maturity quarterly

sections.heading: Ownership Model and RACI
paragraphs: Primary roles
bullets: Executive Sponsor (EX): accountably backs the program and clears roadblocks.
AI Governance Lead (AG): operates the framework, intake, reviews, and reporting.
IT / Platform Owner (IT): manages enablement, access, integration, and platform controls.
Security (SE): enforces security controls, monitoring, and incident coordination.
Privacy (PR): covers personal-data handling, retention, and privacy reviews.
Legal / Compliance (LE): addresses regulatory, contractual, and policy interpretations.
Data Owner / Governance (DA): owns data quality, provenance, and approved access.
Business Owner (BU): owns the use case, benefits, and outcome accountability.
Procurement / Vendor Risk (PC): performs third-party diligence and contract coordination.
Engineering / Product (EN): implements technical safeguards and rollout design.

sections.heading: Sample RACI snapshot
paragraphs: Escalation path: Business Owner -> AI Governance Lead -> Security/Privacy/Legal as needed -> Executive Sponsor for Tier 4 or exception approvals.

sections.heading: AI Use Case Intake and Risk Tiering
Required intake questions
bullets: Business purpose and intended outcome
AI capability type and provider
Internal-only or external/customer-facing use
Data sensitivity and regulated-data exposure
Decision impact and human oversight requirement
Automated actions or tool permissions
Third-party/vendor dependence
Applicable regulations or contractual obligations

sections.heading: Risk tier logic
tables: Business workflow support with moderate data sensitivity
Sensitive data, consequential outputs, or customer impact
Unsafe, disallowed, or materially high-risk use
Block or route to Executive Sponsor

sections.heading: Policy-to-Control Mapping Baseline
paragraphs: Use this baseline to turn policy language into minimum and enhanced operational controls.
tables: Use approved tools only for approved purposes
Approved tools register and owner assignment
Periodic usage review and exception workflow
Do not expose sensitive data without authorization
Material AI activity must be auditable
Alerting, anomaly checks, and periodic review
AI incidents must be escalated rapidly
AI incident categories and owner list

sections.heading: Incident categories
bullets: Harmful or misleading output
Privacy breach or unauthorized data exposure
Security exposure or embedded secret disclosure
Model or agent drift causing unsafe behavior
Unauthorized automation or unapproved integration
Third-party vendor or service failure

sections.heading: Workflow
paragraphs: Minimum evidence: incident summary, owner, timeline, impacted data/systems, containment action, recovery decision, lessons learned.
bullets: Detect and triage
Assess severity and impacted systems
Contain or disable affected AI activity
Escalate to Security, Privacy, Legal, and Executive Sponsor as required
Remediate and restore safe operation
Review root cause, evidence, and control improvements

sections.heading: Governance Glossary
paragraphs: AI system: Any model, tool, agent, workflow, or embedded capability used to generate, classify, retrieve, recommend, or automate decisions.
Agent: An AI-enabled system that can invoke tools, call external systems, or take actions toward a task.
High-impact use case: A use case whose output can materially affect people, finances, legal posture, privacy, or safety.
Human oversight: A defined review or approval action by a responsible human before or after AI output is used.
Model drift: A measurable change in behavior, performance, or output quality over time that can affect reliability or safety.
Shadow AI: Use of AI tools or capabilities outside approved governance, visibility, or control.
Knowledge base: A collection of internal or external documents and data sources used to ground or enrich AI outputs.
Prohibited use: A use case that the organization forbids because the risk cannot be reduced to an acceptable level.

sections.heading: Standards Alignment Crosswalk
paragraphs: Reference only - not a legal certification claim.

sections.heading: AI Governance Evidence Register
bullets: AI policy and approved tools list
Use-case intake records and risk assessments
AI system inventory
Vendor review records
Monitoring logs and exception records
Incident reports and quarterly review decks

GS-PLBK-THIRDPAR-14.title: GovSecure Third-Party AI Privacy Risk Assessment

sections.heading: GovSecure Third-Party AI Privacy Risk Assessment
paragraphs: Vendor Questionnaire and Evidence Review Pack

sections.heading: Purpose
paragraphs: Assess privacy, data protection, governance, and AI data-use risk in third-party AI vendors, deployers, and adopters — with a primary emphasis on vendor accountability.
Designed for use during onboarding, procurement review, renewal review, major product changes, and post-incident reassessment.
Version 1.0
This document includes section numbering, answer fields, evidence-request columns, reviewer risk scoring, red-flag triggers, and an executive summary page for internal reviewers.

sections.heading: Executive Summary for Reviewers
paragraphs: Complete after receiving the vendor response and evidence pack.

sections.heading: Risk Scoring Guidance
bullets: 1 = Informational / low concern
2 = Minor concern / limited relevance
3 = Moderate concern / follow-up needed
4 = High concern / mitigation or leadership review needed
5 = Critical concern / automatic escalation if unresolved

sections.heading: Automatic Red-Flag Triggers
bullets: Customer data is used for training, tuning, or product improvement by default.
Vendor cannot explain retention, deletion scope, or storage locations.
No privacy lead, no privacy program, or no DPA support.
Cross-tenant exposure is possible or not clearly ruled out.
Sensitive or regulated data is permitted without explicit safeguards.
Broad human review occurs without strict controls.
Subprocessors or transfer mechanisms are not disclosed.
Recent privacy or security incident lacks clear remediation.

sections.heading: How to Complete This Questionnaire
paragraphs: Suggested response types: Yes / No; Yes / No / Partial; Select all that apply; Short answer; Long answer; Attachment required; Date.
bullets: Vendor response: complete the 'Answer Field' area for each question or provide a linked workbook response.
Evidence requested: attach or reference the supporting artifact where available.
Reviewer notes / risk lens: reserved for GovSecure or internal reviewers after submission.
Score: reviewer enters 1–5 after evaluating the response and evidence.
Red-flag trigger: indicates conditions that should automatically trigger escalation or conditional approval.

sections.heading: Section 1 — Vendor / Provider Profile
paragraphs: 12 questions
tables: Company registration or legal entity record
No legal entity or ambiguous provider identity
Product being assessed is not clearly identified
Public product page or documentation link
Headquarters or legal jurisdiction cannot be confirmed
Countries where service is delivered or supported
Service region list or support matrix
Material operations occur in high-risk or unknown jurisdictions
Primary business contact name and title
Privacy office or DPO contact details
No privacy contact or escalation channel
No security contact or escalation channel

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Role statement (provider, deployer, integrator, processor, controller, etc.)
Vendor cannot clearly define its legal or operational role
Briefly describe the AI product or service being assessed
Product overview, architecture summary, or model card
Product scope, intended purpose, or AI functionality is unclear
Describe intended business use case(s) for the customer
Use case summary or implementation scope
Use case is open-ended, high impact, or not bounded

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Capability description (GenAI, ML, recommendation, agentic, biometric, profiling, etc.)
High-risk capability is used without supporting controls

sections.heading: Section 2 — AI System Overview
paragraphs: 8 questions
tables: What type of AI model(s) or services are used?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Model architecture summary or model list
Model stack or provider dependencies are not disclosed
Identify all third-party model providers, cloud providers, subprocessors, or data partners involved
Architecture diagram and third-party dependency list
Critical dependencies are withheld or materially incomplete
Does the solution process customer inputs in real time, store them, or both?
Data flow diagram or processing description
Storage or processing behavior is unclear
Does the solution use customer data for inference only, product improvement, model fine-tuning, training, analytics, or human review?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Data use statement and contract terms
Customer data is used for training or improvement by default
Does the AI system make decisions, recommendations, or outputs that could materially affect individuals?
High-impact decision support exists without extra safeguards
If yes, describe the types of impact
Impact assessment or use case classification
Potential effect on employment, access, finance, health, or rights is not evaluated
Is human review required before high-impact outputs or actions are used?
No human review for high-impact outputs or actions
Can the system take actions automatically on behalf of a user or organization?
Autonomous actions exist without scoping or approval thresholds

sections.heading: Section 3 — Personal Data and Privacy Scope
paragraphs: 10 questions
tables: What categories of personal data may be processed by the service?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Data inventory or processing category list
Extensive personal data categories are processed without minimization
What categories of sensitive or regulated data may be processed?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Sensitive data inventory or control statement
Sensitive or regulated data is permitted without explicit safeguards
Is the customer permitted to submit sensitive personal data into the service?
Sensitive data submission is allowed by default
If yes, what controls exist to restrict, mask, filter, classify, or protect that data?
DLP, filtering, classification, masking, or policy evidence
No preventive or detective controls for sensitive data
Does the system collect personal data directly from users, indirectly from customers, or from third-party sources?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Data source paths are not documented
Does the service infer new personal data, traits, profiles, or behavioral insights from submitted data?
Vendor creates inferred profiles without transparency or controls
Does the solution generate content that may contain personal data from training data, retrieval data, or prior prompts?
Potential regeneration of personal data is not tested or addressed
Can the solution return data belonging to another customer, tenant, or user?
Tenant isolation design and testing evidence
Cross-tenant leakage is possible or not ruled out
Describe all data elements required to use the service safely and effectively
Describe any data elements that are optional but commonly submitted by customers
Optional data includes unnecessary or sensitive content

sections.heading: Section 4 — Purpose Limitation and Data Use
paragraphs: 8 questions
tables: Describe the specific purposes for which customer data is processed
Purposes are broad, vague, or not limited
Is customer data used only to deliver the contracted service?
Customer data is used beyond contracted service delivery
Is customer data ever used for training, model improvement, benchmarking, analytics, advertising, profiling, research, or sharing with third parties?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Privacy terms and data use statement
Secondary data use exists without explicit opt-in
Can customers opt out of model training, product improvement, or analytics use of their data?
Admin settings, DPA clause, or product controls
No opt-out for non-essential secondary uses
Are opt-out settings enabled by default or only upon request?
If data is de-identified or anonymized, describe the method and whether re-identification risk is assessed
Are prompts, uploads, outputs, embeddings, logs, or telemetry reused for any secondary purpose?
Prompts or outputs are reused without customer awareness
If yes, describe each secondary purpose clearly
Secondary purpose is ambiguous or materially broad

sections.heading: Section 5 — Data Retention, Deletion, and Disposal
paragraphs: 7 questions
tables: What is the default retention period for prompts, uploads, outputs, histories, embeddings, logs, metadata, and human review content?
Retention periods are not defined or are excessive
Admin guide or product settings evidence
No configurable retention for customer-controlled data
Can customers delete submitted data, prompts, outputs, and logs on demand?
Customer deletion rights are not supported
When data is deleted, is it removed from production, backups, indexes, vector stores, training datasets, fine-tunes, and logs?
Describe deletion timelines and any exceptions
Deletion timing is unclear or materially delayed
If customer data is used in training or fine-tuning, can that data later be removed or unlearned?
No removal path for trained-on customer data
Describe secure disposal procedures at end of contract

sections.heading: Section 6 — Data Sharing, Subprocessors, and Cross-Border Transfers
paragraphs: 9 questions
tables: List all subprocessors involved in hosting, inference, storage, support, analytics, review, or model operations
Do subprocessors receive personal data, prompts, outputs, or metadata?
Data flow diagram or subprocessor data map
Vendor cannot specify what subprocessors receive
Are subprocessors contractually restricted from using customer data for their own purposes?
In which countries is customer data stored, processed, accessed, or supported?
Data residency and support location statement
Storage or access locations are unknown
Transfer map or regional processing statement
If yes, what transfer mechanism is used?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
SCCs, adequacy, UK IDTA, DPF, or equivalent evidence
No lawful transfer mechanism for restricted transfers
Can customers choose data residency or processing region?
Regional hosting options or contract term
No regional choice for sensitive workloads
Can vendor support personnel access customer data from outside the chosen region?
Remote support access bypasses region commitments
Describe controls for international transfers and remote support access
Support access is broad, uncontrolled, or unlogged

sections.heading: Section 7 — Privacy Governance and Accountability
paragraphs: 8 questions
tables: Do you maintain a documented privacy program?
Do you have a designated privacy officer, DPO, or equivalent accountable leader?
Org chart or privacy governance statement
Do you conduct privacy impact assessments, DPIAs, or AI-specific risk assessments for the product?
No privacy impact assessment for material AI processing
Is privacy-by-design formally incorporated into product development?
SDLC control or design review evidence
Are AI product changes subject to privacy review before release?
Material AI changes bypass privacy review
Do you maintain records of processing activities relevant to this service?
Do you have documented policies for privacy, retention, access control, AI governance, training data governance, data subject rights, and incident response?
Core governance policies are missing or immature
Are employees trained on privacy and responsible AI requirements?
Training completion summary or program description
No workforce training on privacy and responsible AI

sections.heading: Section 8 — Data Subject Rights and User Transparency
paragraphs: 7 questions
tables: Can customers fulfill data subject rights requests using your service?
Can personal data be located, exported, corrected, deleted, or restricted upon request?
Does your service support lawful response to access, deletion, correction, portability, objection, or restriction requests?
Rights support is partial with no clear limitations
Are end users informed when they are interacting with AI?
No notice when users interact with AI
Are customers provided with privacy disclosures, notices, or model usage explanations they can reuse?
Customers receive no transparency support materials
Does the system support output traceability or explainability for privacy-relevant outputs?
Audit trail or explainability feature evidence
If the service profiles individuals or supports decisions affecting them, what transparency controls exist?
Profiling or decision support exists without disclosure or challenge path

sections.heading: Section 9 — AI Training, Fine-Tuning, and Model Governance
paragraphs: 10 questions
tables: What sources were used to train the model(s)?
Training data source summary or model card
Were personal data or public internet sources included in training?
Personal data use in training is not disclosed
How was training data screened for privacy, consent, licensing, and appropriateness?
No screening for privacy, consent, or licensing
Are customer prompts, files, outputs, or telemetry used to fine-tune models?
Fine-tuning policy or data use setting
Customer content is used for fine-tuning without explicit authorization
Can customer-specific models or isolated fine-tunes be created?
Customer-specific isolation is unavailable for sensitive use cases
If yes, are those isolated from other customers?
Fine-tunes or models are not isolated
Are training, fine-tuning, or retrieval datasets reviewed for sensitive, inaccurate, or excessive personal data?
No review of privacy risk in datasets
Do you test for memorization, leakage, or regeneration of personal data?
Privacy leakage test results or summary
No testing for memorization or leakage
Do you monitor for model drift or changes that affect privacy risk?
Monitoring procedure or model governance docs
Are model updates communicated to customers before or after deployment?
Material updates are silent or non-transparent

sections.heading: Section 10 — Human Review, Annotation, and Access to Content
paragraphs: 7 questions
tables: Do employees, contractors, reviewers, or annotators access customer prompts, files, or outputs?
Human access exists without transparency or strict controls
If yes, for what purposes are human reviewers used?

Select all that apply

Vendor Response:
________________________
Human access extends beyond necessary purposes
Human access is not role-based or logged
Is access restricted to approved personnel with confidentiality obligations?
Reviewers are not subject to confidentiality and screening
Are human reviewers located in specific countries or regions?
Can customers opt out of human review where not legally or operationally required?
No opt-out for discretionary human review
Describe monitoring and controls around human access to content
Monitoring evidence or admin oversight procedure
Human access monitoring is weak or undocumented

sections.heading: Section 11 — Security Controls Supporting Privacy
paragraphs: 12 questions
tables: Is customer data encrypted in transit and at rest?
No encryption at rest or in transit
Are tenant environments logically or physically segregated?
Tenant segregation is weak or unclear
Are access controls role-based and least-privilege?

Automatic escalation if unresolved. Usually blocks approval or requires executive exception.

sections.heading: Executive Summary
tables: Use this page to summarize the vendor privacy risk posture after reviewing the questionnaire.

sections.heading: Scoring & Red Flags
tables: Notable concern. Follow-up evidence or mitigation needed.
Material concern. Escalate to leadership or condition approval.
• Customer data is used for training, tuning, or product improvement by default.
• Vendor cannot explain retention, deletion scope, or storage locations.
• No privacy lead, no privacy program, or no DPA support.
• Cross-tenant exposure is possible or not clearly ruled out.
• Sensitive or regulated data is permitted without explicit safeguards.
• Broad human review of prompts, files, or outputs occurs without strict controls.
• Subprocessors or transfer mechanisms are not disclosed.
• Recent privacy or security incident lacks clear remediation or transparency.

sections.heading: Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) AI Questionnaire
paragraphs: Purpose: Assess risk for vendors providing AI/ML/GenAI capabilities or AI-enabled features in SaaS.
Vendor: ________ Product: ________ Business Owner: ________ Review Date: ________
Use Case: ☐ Underwriting ☐ Claims ☐ Fraud/SIU ☐ Pricing ☐ Customer Ops ☐ IT/Sec Ops ☐ Other: ________
Deployment: ☐ Vendor-hosted ☐ API ☐ On-prem ☐ Hybrid
Data Types Shared: ☐ None ☐ PII ☐ PHI ☐ PCI ☐ Financial ☐ Minors ☐ Biometrics ☐ Telematics ☐ Other: ________

sections.heading: 1) AI System Overview (Required)
paragraphs: Describe the AI capability (model type, purpose, user workflow).
Is this GenAI/LLM? ☐ Yes ☐ No
Does the AI output materially influence decisions (eligibility, pricing, claims outcome, fraud actions)? ☐ Yes ☐ No
What environments does it run in (prod/dev), and where is it hosted (cloud/region)?
Provide model documentation: ☐ Model Card ☐ System Card ☐ Technical whitepaper ☐ None
Auto-High trigger: If “Yes” to #3 in insurance lines, treat as High Risk.

sections.heading: 2) Data Handling & Privacy (High Importance)
paragraphs: What data is ingested? Is sensitive/regulated data involved (PHI/PCI/minors/biometrics)?
Data flow: where is data stored, processed, and transmitted (regions)?
Retention: default retention for prompts, outputs, logs, and training datasets?
Deletion: can customer data be deleted on request? SLA?
Is customer data used for model training or tuning?
☐ No
☐ Yes (opt-in)
☐ Yes (default)
Are prompts/inputs ever reviewed by vendor staff? Under what controls?
Subprocessors: provide list + change notification process.
Cross-border transfer mechanism (SCCs, etc.) where applicable.
Does the vendor support DPIA support docs / privacy annex?
Required evidence: DPA, data flow diagram, retention schedule, subprocessors list.

sections.heading: 3) Security Controls (High Importance)
paragraphs: Authentication & access: SSO/SAML/OIDC? MFA enforced? Role-based access?
Encryption: in transit and at rest (standards used).
Key management: customer-managed keys (CMK/BYOK) available? ☐ Yes ☐ No
Logging: what events are logged (admin actions, API calls, data access)? Log retention?
Vulnerability management: patch SLAs, scanning, pen-test frequency.
Security certifications/reports: ☐ SOC 2 ☐ ISO 27001 ☐ PCI ☐ Other: ____
Incident response: notification timeline, IR contacts, breach reporting process.
Data segregation: multi-tenant isolation controls.
Backup and DR: RPO/RTO.
Required evidence: SOC2/ISO report, security whitepaper, IR policy, encryption statement.

sections.heading: 4) GenAI / LLM-Specific Controls (If Applicable)
paragraphs: Prompt/response logging: is it enabled? Can customer disable? Redaction options?
Preventing data leakage: how do you stop sensitive data from being returned?
Defense against prompt injection / jailbreaks: controls and testing approach.
Tool use / plugins / connectors:
What connectors exist?
Can tool access be restricted per tenant?
Are there “write actions” possible (send email, update records)?
Content safety: toxic/harmful content filtering?
Hallucination management: guardrails, citations, refusal patterns, reliability controls.
Can outputs be constrained to an approved knowledge base? ☐ Yes ☐ No
Does vendor provide red-teaming results or safety evaluation reports?
Auto-High trigger: Write-enabled tools/connectors + sensitive data.

sections.heading: 5) Model Governance & Change Management
paragraphs: How are model updates deployed (frequency, forced vs optional)?
Advance notice period for major model/version changes?
Can customers pin versions? Roll back?
What testing occurs before changes (regression, safety, bias)?
Explainability: can vendor provide reason codes or transparency for decisions (non-GenAI ML)?
Drift monitoring: do you support drift detection dashboards or alerts?
Required evidence: change management policy, model update communications, versioning controls.

sections.heading: 6) Fairness, Bias, and Consumer Harm (Insurance-Critical)
paragraphs: For underwriting/pricing/claims/fraud: what bias testing is performed?
What protected/proxy attribute controls exist?
How do you support adverse-action style explanations (where applicable)?
Can the system be configured with human review before adverse decisions?
Customer dispute/appeal support: what audit logs exist to investigate outcomes?
Auto-High trigger: models influencing underwriting/pricing/claims outcomes without bias testing + explainability support.

sections.heading: 7) Legal, Contract, and Audit Rights
paragraphs: Contractually confirm: no training on our data unless explicitly approved. ☐ Confirmed ☐ Not confirmed
Audit rights: can you provide evidence on demand (SOC2 bridge letters, control attestations)?
Liability/indemnity: IP infringement, privacy violations, and regulatory exposure (where negotiable).
SLA commitments: uptime, support, incident response.
Termination: data return and secure deletion certification.
Required evidence: contract clauses, DPA, audit report list, deletion attestation.

sections.heading: 8) Operational Controls & Monitoring
paragraphs: Admin controls available to customer: disable features, restrict data types, restrict users.
Monitoring dashboards: usage, anomalies, sensitive data detection.
Exportable logs to SIEM? ☐ Yes ☐ No
Support model: escalation path for AI incidents (harmful output, data leak, bias complaint).

sections.heading: 9) Risk Rating & Decision (Internal)
paragraphs: Risk Tier: ☐ Low ☐ Medium ☐ High
Decision: ☐ Approve ☐ Conditional Approve ☐ Reject
Conditions / Required Remediations:

sections.heading: Page 1
paragraphs:
GovSecure | 3rd-Party AI Vendor Risk Review
GOVSECURE | GOVERNANCE WORKFLOW AND ADD-ON TOOL
3rd-Party AI Vendor Risk Review
Structured due diligence and decision workbook for reviewing AI vendors, copilots,
embedded AI features, and AI-enabled SaaS before approval.
Prepared For {{COMPANY_NAME}}
Version / Effective Date v1.0 | {{EFFECTIVE_DATE}}
Pre-procurement, renewal, major feature
Primary Use
enablement, or control revalidation
Purpose Use this workbook to evaluate third-party AI products and AI-enabled vendor
features with a standard, defensible review method. It is built for SMBs that buy AI more
often than they build it, and need practical evidence before saying yes.
This workbook should be retained as the internal review record. The decision summary page can be
shared with procurement, leadership, security, legal, or business owners as needed.
1. Review Overview
This review is designed to answer a simple question: can this vendor be trusted to support the
intended AI use case at an acceptable level of risk? It gives the organization a repeatable
structure for assessing security, privacy, legal, operational, and AI-specific concerns before
approval.

activation, or control

refresh.
2. Standard Decision Outcomes
• Approve - vendor is acceptable for the reviewed use case under normal operating controls.
